The book 'Anatomy of Movement' was originally published in French as 'Anatomie pour le mouvement' by Editions Desiris in 1985.
The English language edition of 'Anatomy of Movement' was published in 1993 and 2007 by Eastland Press.
The book was translated by Regine MacKenzie.
The book was edited by John O'Connor and Allan Kaplan.
The cover illustration was done by Sandy Johnson.
The cover design was done by Patricia O'Connor.
The book design was done by Gary Niemeier.
This book is an introductory text on basic human anatomy as it relates to external body movement.
The book focuses on bones, muscles, and joints.
The book does not describe the skull, visceral organs, circulatory system, or central nervous system.
The book is designed to be compact and avoid repetition.
Parts of the body affected by the same muscles may be described together.
Reference may be given to a previous page where a structure or function is described in more detail.
Drawings usually show structures from the right side of the body, with exceptions clearly indicated.
Joints are sometimes drawn without adjacent bones to show articular surfaces.
Each muscle is drawn in isolation to make its function more obvious.
Chapter 1 provides basic orientation and terminology and should be read first.
Subsequent chapters are arranged in a logical order (trunk, arm, leg) and should be read in order.
The index is helpful for locating the page where a structure is first mentioned or described in detail.
The anatomy of movement in the human body mainly involves the interaction of bones, joints, and muscles.
Description of movements can be difficult because various body parts can move in many directions and often more than one joint is involved.
For consistency, each joint is considered in isolation, three perpendicular planes are used for reference, and movements are described in relation to the anatomical position.
The anatomical position is standing upright, feet parallel, arms hanging by the sides, palms and face directed forward.
Flexion of the wrist is a movement that takes the hand forward from the anatomical position.
The median plane divides the body into symmetrical right and left halves.
Any plane parallel to the median plane is called a sagittal plane.
Movements in the sagittal plane can be seen from the side.
A movement in the sagittal plane that takes a body part forward from the anatomical position is called flexion.
Examples of flexion include flexion of the hip and flexion of the shoulder.
An exception to flexion is dorsiflexion of the ankle.
A movement in the sagittal plane that takes a body part backward from the anatomical position is called extension.
Examples of extension include extension of the neck and extension of the shoulder.
Exceptions to extension include plantarflexion of the ankle and flexion of the knee.
A coronal or frontal plane is any plane perpendicular to the median plane.
A coronal or frontal plane divides the body into anterior and posterior parts.
A movement in a frontal plane which takes a part of the body toward the median plane is called adduction.
Movements on the frontal plane can be seen from the front.
Example: adduction of the hip.
For the trunk or neck, movement in the frontal plane away from the median plane is called lateral flexion or sidebending.
Example: right lateral flexion of the trunk.
The opposite type of movement (away from the median plane) is called abduction.
Example: abduction of the shoulder.
For the fingers or toes, the reference used is the axis of the hand (middle finger) or foot (2d toe).
Example: abduction of the fifth finger moves it away from the axis of the hand (not from the median plane).
A transverse plane divides the body into superior and inferior (upper and lower) parts.
Movements in this plane can be seen from the top or bottom.
A movement in a transverse plane which takes a part of the body outward is called lateral rotation.
Example: lateral rotation of the hip (the part being moved here is the front of the thigh).
The opposite type of movement (inward) is called medial rotation.
Example: medial rotation of the shoulder (the part being moved is the front of the upper arm).
In supination of the forearm, the palm faces upward or forward.
For the trunk or neck, we refer simply to right or left rotation.
The reference point is the front of the chest or head.
In pronation of the forearm, the palm of the hand faces downward or backward.
Complex body movements typically involve movement in all three planes.
Example: sitting in the 'tailor's position' involves flexion, abduction, and lateral rotation of the hip joints.
Medial: closer to the median plane.
Lateral: further from the median plane.
Example: medial and lateral surfaces of the humerus.
Anterior: facing toward or located at the front.
Posterior: facing toward or located at the back.
Example: anterior and posterior surfaces of the forearm.
Superior: facing toward or located at the top (closer to the head).
Inferior: facing toward or located at the bottom (further from the head).
Example: superior and inferior ends of the femur.
Superficial (or external) on or near the outside (external) surface of the body or a particular bone or organ.
Deep (or internal): inside the body or a particular bone or organ.
Example: deep and superficial layer of the gastrocnemius muscle.
Proximal: closer to the trunk, or some major joint.
Distal: further from the trunk, or some major joint.
Example: the articulations between bones of the fingers are called proximal and distal interphalangeal joints.
The bones act as levers and points of attachment for the muscles.
Bone tissue consists of about two-thirds mineral components (mostly calcium salts), which give rigidity, and one-third organic components, which give elasticity.

Bones are subjected to several types of mechanical strain: gravitational pressure from the body itself, movement (muscle contraction) against resistance, e.g., lifting a heavy object, and twisting forces.
The skeleton is a mobile framework of bones providing rigid support for the body.
The bones also serve as levers for the action of muscles.
There are three basic shapes of bones: short bones such as the talus (ankle bone), flat bones such as the scapula, and long bones such as the radius and ulna.
Bones (particularly those of the feet, legs, and back) must support the weight of the body.
Gravitational pressure from external objects (traction), e.g., supporting a heavy suitcase, is a type of mechanical strain bones are subjected to.
A long bone, in this case the femur, consists of three parts: the central shaft called the diaphysis, and the two ends called the epiphyses.
The diaphysis is a hollow tube with walls made of compact bone.
The hollow structure of the diaphysis gives light weight and is actually sturdier than a solid structure would be.
Compact bone is thickest in the middle section of the diaphysis where mechanical strains are greatest.
Compact bone also predominates in the concave areas of the curved ends.
A cross section of the epiphysis shows a trabecular (spongy) structure.
Fibers are arranged in rows along the lines of greatest mechanical stress in the trabecular structure.
The hollow part of the diaphysis contains the bone marrow.
The marrow is red in children, but becomes yellow in adults as much as it is replaced by fatty tissue.
The external surface of the bone is covered with a membrane called the periosteum.
The articular surfaces of the bones are covered with articular (hyaline) cartilage.
Joints are areas where bones are linked together.
Joints have varying degrees of mobility.
In some joints the bones are linked simply by fibrous connective tissue or cartilage, allowing little or no movement.
Our primary interest will be in freely-movable joints called diarthroses, or synovial joints.
The surfaces (sometimes called facets) of synovial joints are shaped so as to fit together but also allow movement.
There are many general categories of joints, based on the shape of the articulating surfaces.
In a ball-and-socket joint, a ball-like surface of one bone fits into a cup-like depression of another bone.
This type of joint, also called an enarthrodial or spheroidal joint, allows movement in all directions.
Examples of ball-and-socket joints include the shoulder.
In an ellipsoid joint, the shallow cavity of one bone receives the rounded surface of another.
This allows movement in all three planes described on pages 8-10.
Examples of ellipsoid joints include the metacarpophalangeal joint.
In a hinge joint, the convex surface of one bone fits against the concave surface of another.
Movement is chiefly in one plane.
Examples of hinge joints include the ankle.
In a pivot joint, the rounded surface of one bone fits into a ring formed partly by another bone.
Movement is chiefly in one direction, like the hinge of a door.
Examples of pivot joints include the radioulnar joint.
Both surfaces of a saddle are saddle-shaped, convex in one direction and concave in the other.
This saddle-shaped structure permits movement in two planes.
The articulating surfaces in a joint do not always make a snug fit.
Some joints are more stable than others.
The ball-and-socket structure of the hip is deep and snug-fitting.
The ball-and-socket of the shoulder is shallow, looser, and less stable.
Between the articulating ends of the two bones in a joint is a gap which is not opaque to X-rays.
This gap corresponds to the articular cartilages and synovial cavity.
When you look at an X-ray, you can only see the thickness of the cartilages in the joint.
The cartilages themselves are not visible on X-ray.
Thus, it looks like there is a free space between the two bones.
In a dislocation or subluxation, a bone is moved from its normal position in a joint because of some trauma.
There is associated damage to ligaments, etc.
Articulating surfaces of bones are covered with a shiny, whitish connective tissue called cartilage.
Cartilage contributes to the synovial capsule and also protects the underlying bone tissue.
Cartilage is well-adapted to stresses, being strong, resilient, and smooth.
Thus, it can allow some sliding of the bones relative to each other.

When movement occurs, joint cartilage is subjected to two types of stress: gravitational pressure (particularly in the weight-bearing joints of the legs and feet) and friction from the movement itself.
Nevertheless, cartilage may be damaged either by trauma or excessive wear (e.g., when the ends of the articulating bones do not provide a good 'fit').
Rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis are two common diseases involving damage to joint cartilage, accompanied by inflammation, pain, and stiffness of the joint and surrounding muscles.
Joint cartilage (like all cartilage) does not contain blood vessels.
It receives nutrients from the synovial fluid and the bone it covers.
Fibrocartilage contains high concentrations of collagenous (white) fibers and is specially adapted for absorbing shock.
Fibrocartilage is found in the intervertebral discs and in the menisci (articular discs) of the knee and other large joints.
The joint capsule encloses the joint, prevents loss of fluid, and binds together the ends of the articulating bones.
The outer layer of the capsule is composed of dense connective tissue and represents a continuation of the periosteum.
The joint capsule is stronger where movement must be prevented.
Fibers of the outer capsule are often arranged in parallel bundles (called ligaments; see following section) to reinforce joints and prevent unwanted movement.
The capsule may be arranged loosely or in folds where movement is possible.
The interior of the capsule is covered by a synovial membrane, which covers the deep surface of the capsule and folds over at the capsular insertions.
Its principal function is to secrete synovial fluid that fills the articular cavity.
This fluid lubricates the joint and provides nutrients to the cartilage.
Ligaments are dense bundles of parallel collagenous fibers.
Ligaments are often derived from the outer layer of the joint capsule, but may also connect nearby but non-articulating bones.
The ligaments connecting the sacrum to the spine and tuberosity of the ischium is an example of a ligament.
Ligaments function chiefly to strengthen and stabilize the joint in a passive way.
Unlike muscles, ligaments cannot actively contract.
Except for a few ligaments which contain a high proportion of yellow elastic fibers, ligaments also cannot stretch.
The fibular (lateral) collateral ligament of the knee is pulled tight in extension and loosened in flexion.
Ligaments contain numerous sensory nerve cells capable of responding to the speed, movement, and position of the joint, as well as to stretching or pain.
Sensory cells in ligaments constantly transmit information to the brain, which in turn sends signals to the muscles via motor neurons.
This is called the proprioceptive sense.
Excessive movement of the joint can lead to ligamentous stretching to the point of straining or tearing the ligament.
This is called a sprain.
Essentially all movements of the human body result from contraction of muscles.
This book focuses on skeletal muscles (also known as voluntary or striated muscles) which attach to bones.

In a microscopic cross section, a muscle is composed of bundles of fibers (primary, secondary, tertiary), held together and compartmentalized by fibrous partitions called deep fascia, epimysium, perimysium, and endomysium.
They can be extended beyond the muscle to form a strong fibrous cord called a tendon which is continuous with the periosteum of a nearby bone, and serves to attach the muscle to the bone.
A broad, flattened tendon is called an aponeurosis.
These connective tissue partitions (which are continuous with each other) allow easy movement of one muscle or muscle group relative to another.
Each muscle cell, also called a muscle fiber, contains extremely long myofibrils.
Each myofibril contains a contractile element in its central part, the sarcomere.

The structure of the sarcomere appears (under extreme magnification) to consist of filaments: dark bundles, thick filaments, which are rounded in the middle (consisting of myosin, a type of protein), and light bundles, thin filaments, linked to each other through their central part, consisting of actin (another type of protein).
At rest, the actin and myosin filaments are not connected.
When the muscle contracts, they unite, and pull each other.
This causes a thickening (diameter) and shortening (length), which makes it possible for the muscle to pull the bones to which it is attached.
Typically, a muscle is attached to two different bones.
For a given body movement, one bone (called the origin) is fixed in some way, and the other (called the insertion) moves as a result of muscle contraction.
The origin is often the proximal bone, and the insertion the distal bone, but there are many exceptions.
For example, the gluteus medius connects the ilium (large hip bone) to the femur.
If the ilium is fixed, contraction of this muscle results in abduction of the femur.
If one is standing with the weight on the leg, the femur becomes the fixed point, and contraction of the muscle results in lateral flexion of the pelvis.
In this book, we will describe muscle actions where the proximal attachment is the fixed point.
For some muscles or regions, we will add the muscle action where the distal attachment is the fixed point.
Besides their (active) ability to contract, muscles have a (passive) property of elasticity.
When stretched, they tend to return to their normal resting length.
For example, the anterior neck muscles, when they contract, are flexors of the neck.
During extension of the neck, these muscles become stretched.
When this happens, because of their elasticity, they tend to return the head to its anatomical position.

Muscles attach to bones in several ways: directly via muscle fibers (usually in a broad insertion), via an aponeurosis (broad tendon), via a regular tendon, and via the tendon passing under a fibrous band.
Some muscles have several origins (called "heads"), which may be on more than one bone.
Example: biceps brachii has two heads, triceps brachii has three heads, and quadriceps femoris has four heads.
Typically, the proximal insertion of the muscle is called origin and the distal one is called insertion.
Example: the psoas muscle has its origin on the vertebrae and its insertion on the femur.

A muscle can have several origins, Example: flexor digitorum superficialis originates from both the radius and ulna, and several insertions. Example: the interosseous muscles insert on the phalanges and extensor tendons of fingers.
The fiber bundles of the trapezius muscle are arranged in a flattened and fan-shaped manner.
The deep back muscles are short and appear as small bundles along the vertebral column.
The biceps brachii muscle is shaped like a long spindle.
Muscles may act in one or several directions depending on the orientation and attachment of their fibers.
The fibers of the rectus abdominis muscle run essentially parallel to each other and flex the trunk.
The fibers of the external oblique muscle are arranged like a fan and can produce anterior flexion, sidebending, or rotation of the trunk.
Long muscles are usually kinetic, meaning they are able to produce highly visible external motion.
Short, deep muscles tend to be responsible for precise, small-scale adjustments rather than gross movements.
A muscle which crosses and affects a single joint is called monoarticular.
A muscle which crosses (and moves) more than one joint is called polyarticular.
To stretch a muscle, you move it in a manner that is the opposite of its usual action around each of its joints.
The rectus femoris muscle crosses the hip and knee joints and is both a flexor of the hip and an extensor of the knee.
The rectus femoris muscle will be stretched in situations involving simultaneous extension of the hip and flexion of the knee.
The muscle which produces a particular movement is called the agonist, and the muscle which produces the opposite movement is called the antagonist.
Mutually opposing muscles often function together to fix or stabilize a bone.
Different muscles which cooperate to produce the same action are called synergetic.
Three muscles work synergetically in dorsiflexion of the ankle: tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum longus.
In hip flexion, the psoas major is the agonist and the gluteus maximus (a hip extensor) is the antagonist.
The serratus anterior and rhomboids have opposite actions; they protract and retract the scapula, respectively, i.e., move it away from and toward the vertebral column.
By contracting at the same time, the serratus anterior and rhomboids work together to fix the scapula.
When a muscle contracts, it tends to draw its origin and insertion points closer together.
Anything that opposes this tendency is called resistance.
The brachialis and biceps brachii are the major flexors of the elbow.

Resistance to the action of the brachialis and biceps brachii can be provided by the weight of the forearm, the weight of an external object attached to the forearm, the force of another person pulling on the arm, or the contraction of antagonist muscles (in this case the triceps brachii, the major elbow extensor).
When a muscle contracts, a movement occurs.
The rectus abdominis flexes the trunk, moving the sternum toward the pubis.
In the supine position, the rectus abdominis flexes the trunk while the weight of the trunk resists the flexion.
In a standing position, the rectus abdominis is not active, and gravity causes the trunk to fall forward.
When a movement occurs because of an active muscle, the muscle insertions are moved closer together. This type of contraction is called concentric.
In the supine position, a concentric contraction of the trunk flexors occurs.
Two people (A and B) pulling each other (elbows are flexed) results in A winning, which is a concentric contraction of A's elbow flexors.
In some cases, a muscle works without initiating the action itself; instead, it 'applies the brakes' to the action.
Without the work of this muscle braking the action, it would occur faster.
When A 'loses' and brakes the pull of B, an eccentric contraction of A's elbow flexors occurs.
In the standing position, the trunk flexors do not initiate flexion; gravity does.
If the muscles didn't work, flexion would consist of a 'falling' forward.
To slow down flexion, the extensors of the trunk must contract in order to restrain or 'brake' this flexion.
If the muscles which oppose a movement apply the brakes, their contraction is called eccentric. This occurs during lengthening of the muscles.

When the rectus femoris and hamstring muscles combine their actions by flexing the hip and knee (e.g., when squatting or doing a 'grand plie'), the bones involved change their position, but the muscles do not lengthen or shorten.
The hip and knees 'cancel out' each other's actions.
It is possible for a muscle to be contracted even without movement.
The thigh can be flexed (concentric contraction of the hip flexors) and then held in this position.
When a position is fixed by a muscular contraction, we call this contraction isometric.
During an isometric contraction, the insertions of the muscles do not move.
In real life, different types of muscle contractions are combined during various movements.

If we apply the illustration above to the knee, the following would happen when stretching the knee: isometric contraction at the hip flexors and isotonic (concentric) contraction of the knee extensors.
The trunk is the central part of the body.
This book will examine only the locomotor functions of the trunk, not its internal organs.
The trunk serves a double function, which is connected to its bony structure, the vertebral column.
The trunk can bend and perform curved movements, like those of a serpent or a measuring tape.
The mobility of the trunk is due to the flexibility of the vertebral column, which has twenty-six 'levels' of articulation.
The vertebral column contains a tunnel for the nerves: the spinal cord and the nerve roots, which exit the spinal cord.
Weakness in the vertebrae may therefore affect not only the joints, but also the spinal cord and nerves.
The trunk must be able to align and stabilize the vertebral segments when the body is motionless, and especially when it is carrying a load.

This dual function depends on a finely integrated system of mostly polyarticular muscles, which are either deep (composed of numerous small bundles) or superficial (usually arranged like broad sheets).
Movements of the pelvis are difficult to separate from those of the vertebral column.
The trunk can move anteriorly (flexion), laterally (lateral flexion or sidebending), and on its own axis (rotation).
Range of movement of the trunk varies depending on vertebral level.
The shape of the vertebrae, thickness of intervertebral discs, and articulation of thoracic vertebrae with ribs influence trunk movement.
The trunk can move posteriorly (extension).
Do not confuse trunk movements with those in which the trunk moves as a single unit at the hip joint.
Trunk movement can be a secondary consequence of limb movement.
Abduction of the arm takes the trunk into lateral flexion.
Front-to-back or side-to-side movements of the head and pelvis while using a hula-hoop involve trunk movement.
Often, two or more trunk movements are performed in combination.
Examples of combined trunk movements include rotation, extension, and lateral flexion.
The vertebral column (or spine) forms a mobile bony stem that is part of the trunk skeleton.
The spine consists of the cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacrum, and coccyx regions.
There are seven cervical vertebrae, twelve thoracic vertebrae, five lumbar vertebrae, the sacrum, and the coccyx.
Vertebrae are numbered sequentially from top to bottom and are referred to by a letter plus a number.
The exact form of vertebral column curvatures varies from person to person.
Kyphosis is almost non-existent in some individuals.
The external appearance of vertebral column curvatures can be affected by overlying soft structures.
A person with large buttocks may appear to have more pronounced lordosis than a person with small buttocks.
X-rays could reveal identical lumbar curvatures.
The vertebral column is attached to the base of the cranium, the ribs, and the pelvis (ilium).

There are several characteristic curvatures of the vertebral column: the sacrum is convex toward the back, the lumbar region is concave, the thoracic region is convex, and the cervical region is concave.
Each vertebra consists of two main parts: the massive body (anterior) and the vertebral arch (posterior).
The shape and size of a vertebra vary depending on its position in the spine.
The vertebral arch can be divided into many parts and is connected to the body by two pedicles.
Two lamina unite posteriorly to form a spinous process.
The vertebral body is roughly cylindrical and consists of six surfaces.
The opening between the body and the arch is called the vertebral foramen.
The foramina of many vertebrae lined up form the vertebral canal through which the spinal cord passes.
The thickened junctions between the pedicles and laminae have superior and inferior cartilaginous articular facets and a laterally-projecting transverse process.
The vertebral holes stacked on top of each other form a bony pipe called the spinal canal, through which the spinal cord passes.
The spaces between the pedicles of adjacent vertebrae form a series of openings called intervertebral foramina.
As spinal nerves branch off the spinal cord, they exit through the intervertebral foramina.
Each vertebra is attached to its neighbor by three joints (except for the atlas-axis joint).
Anteriorly, the bodies are joined by the fibrocartilaginous intervertebral disc.
Posteriorly, between the articular processes of the vertebrae are the zygapophyseal joints.
The two inferior articular facets of the top vertebra contact the two superior articular facets of the bottom vertebra.
The articular facets are small and mainly serve to guide movements.
The facets are covered with cartilage and held together by a capsule and a number of small ligaments.
The intervertebral disc contains two distinct types of material: the annulus fibrosus and the nucleus pulposus.
The annulus fibrosus is composed of concentric rings of fibrocartilage arranged like the layers of an onion.
The nucleus pulposus is made of a gelatinous substance.
The disc allows movement between vertebrae, acts as a shock absorber, and bears weight.
There are three ligaments extending the length of the vertebral column.
The supraspinous ligament runs along the tips of the spinous processes and acts as a brake to flexion.
The posterior longitudinal ligament is attached to the back of the vertebral bodies and absorbs the thrust from the disc nuclei in flexion.
The anterior longitudinal ligament is attached to the front of the vertebral bodies and acts as a brake to extension.
The other ligaments are discontinuous and hold the posterior arches of the individual vertebrae together.
Interspinous and intertransverse ligaments connect the spinous and transverse processes.
The surfaces of the articular processes are linked together through a capsule insetted on its circumference.
The inside of the capsule is reinforced by an extension of the ligamentum flavum and, at the back, by a posterior ligament.
These ligaments are elastic and can be pierced by a syringe during a spinal tap.
Left sidebending stretches the right intertransverse ligaments.
The ligamentum flavum connects the laminae of adjacent vertebrae.
We can think of the vertebral column as a series of fixed segments (the vertebrae) having mobile connections (discs, ligaments).
Movements of individual vertebrae are compounded such that the entire structure has considerable mobility in three dimensions.
Type and extent of mobility varies with different spinal regions, depending on size and shape of vertebrae, and other factors.
In flexion, the top vertebra (B) tilts toward the front.
The superior articular facets slide on the inferior ones in flexion.
The disc is compressed anteriorly and expanded posteriorly in flexion.
The various parts of the vertebral arches are pulled apart in flexion, and the ligaments connecting these parts are stretched in flexion.
In extension, the opposite occurs. B tilts toward the back.
The disc is compressed posteriorly and expanded anteriorly in extension.
The nucleus of the disc moves forward in extension.
The articular facets are pressed together in extension.
The arches move closer together in extension.
The posterior ligaments are relaxed in extension.
The anterior longitudinal ligament is stretched in extension.
Lateral flexion involves the left sides of vertebrae A and B moving closer together.
On the left side of the spine during lateral flexion, the transverse processes and articular facets come closer together, and the associated ligaments relax.
During left lateral flexion, the right side of the spine moves farther apart.
The disc expands to the right side during left lateral flexion, and its nucleus moves to the right.
The opposite movements occur on the right side during right lateral flexion.
In rotation, the fibers of the intervertebral disc are under torsion (twisting).
The fibers of the intervertebral disc alternate in orientation from one layer to the next.
One layer of the intervertebral disc is stretched while the next layer is relaxed during rotation.
Rotation diminishes the distance between the vertebrae.
The nucleus of the intervertebral disc is slightly compressed during rotation.
All connecting ligaments are stretched by rotation.
The intervertebral disc serves as a shock absorber.
The disc often receives pressure from the vertebral body above.
The nucleus of the intervertebral disc distributes pressure it receives in every direction due to its central location and gelatinous composition.
The fibers of the annulus receive both horizontal and vertical pressures.
As long as fluid remains in the nucleus, the disc performs its role as a shock absorber very efficiently.
The disc may partially lose its shock-absorbing property due to the aging process and/or excessive wear and tear.
Cracks develop in the annulus through which the fluid of the nucleus can escape.
A herniated or ruptured disc occurs when the fluid of the nucleus escapes.
A herniated disc happens most commonly as a result of chronic flexion movements.
During chronic flexion movements, the nucleus moves toward the back, and fluid can escape.
Fluid from a herniated disc may compress nerve roots, such as the sciatic nerve.
Chronic or sudden extreme tension on the posterior longitudinal ligament can result in chronic lumbar backaches.
It is important to avoid 'loaded' vertebral flexion, such as flexing the lumbar spine while lifting a heavy object.
It is preferable to avoid loaded lumbar flexions in any type of physical exercise, even if you are not lifting any object.
Keep the spine straight and flex at the hip and knee joints only.
The pelvis (or pelvic girdle) is a ring-shaped structure.
The pelvis consists of four main bones: the sacrum, two hipbones, and the coccyx.
The pelvis receives the weight of the upper body and passes this weight on to the lower limbs via its articulations with the femurs.
The pelvis must absorb stresses from the lower limbs, such as in walking or jumping.
The shape of the bones forms a greater (false) pelvis at the top and a lesser (true) pelvis at the bottom.
The top opening of the lesser pelvis is called the pelvic inlet.
The hip bones are flat and consist of a superior and inferior part, which are twisted against each other.
In the adult, each hip consists of three bones that are fused together: the ilium, ischium, and pubis.
These bones fuse at a cartilage in the shape of a Y, located in the center of the socket.
The hip has two surfaces (medial and lateral) and four edges (superior, inferior, anterior, and posterior).
On the lateral surface of the hip bone, we find: • in the middle is an area in the form of a hollow sphere, called the acetabulum, which receives the head of the femur.
The superior border or iliac crest is located on the lateral surface of the hip bone.
The lower part of the lateral surface of the hip bone is like a bony arch, which surrounds a hole called the obturator foramen.
The anterior area of the lateral surface of the hip bone is the pubis, and the posterior area is the ischium.
The ischio-pubic ramus is located between the ischium and pubis.

The anterior border of the hip bone has a number of depressions and protuberances, especially: the anterior superior iliac spine, the most forward part of the iliac crest, the anterior inferior iliac spine, and the pubic tubercle.
The top part of the anterior border of the hip bone, which is concave from bottom to top, is the external iliac fossa.

Seen from a three-quarter posterior view, we find the posterior border of the hip bone, which shows various protuberances and depressions, notably: the ischial tuberosity, lesser sciatic notch, ischial spine, greater sciatic notch, posterior inferior iliac spine, and posterior superior iliac spine.
The ischial tuberosity is the bone on which you sit.
The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains the internal iliac fossa.
The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains an oblique crest.
The iliopectineal line, located on the medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis, forms the border between the lesser and greater pelvis.
The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains the internal circumference of the obturator foramen.
The medial surface of the hip bones of the pelvis contains an articular surface located in front of the pubis, in the shape of an oval covered by cartilage.
The pubic symphyseal surface is the articular surface located in front of the pubis, in the shape of an oval covered by cartilage, which unites the two pubic bones.
The pubic symphysis is a joint with very little mobility.
Only small gliding, compression, and twisting movements are possible at the pubic symphysis.
The pubic symphysis loosens during childbirth so that the pelvis can open up further.
The articulation between the two pubic bones is called the pubic symphysis.
Between the two surfaces of the pubic symphysis is a fibrocartilage disc which attaches to the articular surfaces.
The entire pubic symphysis structure is covered by a fibrous cuff, reinforced by four ligaments: anterior, posterior, superior, and inferior.
The shape and proportions of the pelvis vary considerably in normal individuals.
The curve of the sacrum may be more or less emphasized.
The hip bones may be more or less developed.
The gap between the two ischial tuberosities may be bigger or smaller.
The sacral crest or posterior superior iliac spines may protrude in some individuals.

Lack of 'padding' by muscles and adipose tissue, combined with the protrusion of the sacral crest or posterior superior iliac spines, can result in difficulty doing floor exercises, particularly rolling on a hard surface.
There are obvious differences in pelvic shape between males and females.
The male pelvis is narrower.
The female pelvis is wider, with a larger pelvic inlet and outlet.
The differences in pelvic shape between males and females are all related to the ability of women to carry and deliver a child.
The pelvis is sometimes also called the pelvic girdle.
Anatomically, girdles are bony and articular structures by which the limbs are attached to the trunk.
The shoulder girdle, located at the top of the trunk, consists of the sternum, the two clavicles, and the two scapulae.
The shoulder girdle attaches the upper limbs to the trunk.
The shoulder girdle is characterized by its mobility.
The shoulder girdle is not linked via joints to the spinal column, but instead is linked to the thoracic cage.
The pelvic girdle, or pelvis, is located at the bottom of the trunk and consists of the sacrum and the two hip bones.
The pelvic girdle attaches the lower limbs to the trunk.
The pelvic girdle is a very stable structure because the articulations between its bones are not very mobile.
The pelvic girdle is attached to the trunk via the sacro-lumbar joint, which connects it to the spinal column.
The sacral promontory is the upper, anterior edge of S1.
The sacral promontory, together with the iliopectineal lines, defines the boundary between the greater and lesser pelvis.
The anterior surface of the sacrum is smooth and concave.
There are four transverse ridges on the anterior surface of the sacrum, which represent intervertebral discs.
The superior surface of S1, called the base of the sacrum, articulates with L5.
The inferior surface of S5, which articulates with the coccyx, is called the apex of the sacrum.
At the ends of each ridge on the sacrum are paired anterior sacral foramina (corresponding to intervertebral foramina), through which the anterior branches of the sacral nerves pass.
The sacrum is the posterior, wedge-shaped component of the pelvic ring, located between the two ilia.
The sacrum is composed of five expanded, fused vertebrae (S1-S5) whose individual components are readily visible.
The convex posterior surface of the coccyx is much rougher.
The posterior sacral foramina are continuous with the anterior foramina; posterior branches of the sacral nerves exit here.
The median sacral crest and paired lateral sacral crests represent the spinous and transverse processes of the vertebrae.
The spinal cord enters through the sacral canal.
The auricular ('ear-shaped') surfaces of the sacrum are best seen in side view.
The auricular surfaces of the sacrum articulate with the auricular surfaces of the ilia.
The coccyx is a small triangular bone consisting of the fusion of three to five vertebrae.
The coccyx articulates with the sacrum via an oval-shaped surface.
The coccyx is supported by a capsule and ligaments (this joint is often fused).
The sacroiliac joint consists of the two auricular surfaces on top of the ilium and the sacrum.
The auricular surface of the sacrum is slightly concave.
The auricular surface of the hip bone is slightly convex.
The arrangement of the auricular surfaces allows for an important movement between the sacrum and the two ilia, called nutation and counternutation.
In nutation, the sacral base tilts anteroinferiorly, and the sacral apex tilts postefosuperiorly.
During nutation, the sacral promontory moves toward the pubis, and the sacral apex moves away from the pubis, while the ischial bones move away from each other, increasing the distance between them.
In one type of movement, called 'adduction' of the pelvis, the sacrum goes into nutation, the iliac 'wings' are pulled medially, and the ischial tuberosities move laterally.
During adduction, the diameter of the pelvic outlet increases and the pelvic inlet decreases from front to back, especially in the lower portion.
In the movement called 'abduction' of the pelvis, the sacrum moves in counternutation as the sacral base tilts backward and upward and the sacral apex tilts forward and downward.
During abduction, the iliac wings move away from the midline and the ischial bones move toward each other.
During abduction, the pelvic inlet becomes larger from front to back, and the diameter of the pelvic outlet becomes smaller.
Dimensional changes between the pelvic inlet and outlet occur especially during childbirth.
At the beginning of the baby's engagement in the pelvis, there will be a sacral counternutation, and when the baby comes out (expulsion), a nutation.
Each sacroiliac joint is reinforced by a capsule and a strong network of ligaments.
Anteriorly, there are two fasciae (not shown).
Inferiorly, the sacrospinous and sacrotuberous ligaments connect the sacrum to the ischial spine and ischial tuberosity respectively.
These ligaments tend to oppose adduction of the pelvis.
There are also a series of posterior sacroiliac ligaments connecting the ilium to the lateral sacral crest.
These ligaments tend to oppose abduction of the pelvis.
The transverse processes are long and have distinct tubercles on the end for muscle attachment.
The lumbar spine is the next structure above the sacrum.
It is concave at the back.
This is the 'loin' area between the pelvis and the ribcage.
The lumbar vertebrae are large and become larger at the bottom of the lumbar spine.
The discs are thick, about one-third the thickness of the vertebral bodies.
This increases mobility.
The bodies of the vertebrae are large and shaped like a lima bean.
They are concave posteriorly.
The articular processes project above and below the vertebral body, with a narrow part in-between: the isthmus.
The superior articular processes of the vertebrae have a concave cylindrical form.
They point medially (and slightly backward).
The inferior articular processes have a convex cylindrical form.
They point laterally (and slightly forward).
These bony projections articulate with the neighboring vertebrae and are stacked on top of each other.
Their articular facets are vertical and somewhat sagittal.
These characteristics make rotation very difficult (below), but facilitate movements of flexion, extension, and sidebending.
The top lumbar vertebrae are sagittal. They become more and more frontal towards the lower lumbar vertebrae.
At the lumbosacral junction, they are completely frontal.
To summarize the mobility of the lumbar vertebrae: good range of motion for flexion, extension, and sidebending, limited ROM for rotation.
The lumbosacral joint is located between the sacrum and the 5th lumbar vertebra.
The sacral base is tilted forward to a variable degree.
The body of L5 and the disc between L5 and S1 are slightly thicker anteriorly than posteriorly.
The L4/L5 joint also has a thicker anterior portion.
Due to the oblique orientation of the sacrum, there are two perpendicular forces resulting from the weight of the upper body at L5.
One force acts along the axis of the sacrum.
The other force tends to make L5 slide forward.
The contact between the articular facets of S1 and the inferior articular processes of L5 opposes the forward sliding tendency of L5.
The lumbosacral joint is concave posteriorly.
The ligament from L4 is oriented posteroinferiorly.
The ligament from L5 runs more anteroinferiorly.
Iliolumbar ligaments connect the transverse processes of L5 and L4 to the iliac crest.
The iliolumbar ligaments tend to oppose sidebending.
The L4 ligament is stretched and the L5 ligament relaxed during flexion.
The reverse occurs during extension.
The thoracic spine articulates with the ribs.
The thoracic spine consists of twelve vertebrae, the thoracic vertebrae.
The thickness of the discs in the thoracic region is only about one-sixth that of the bodies.
This limits the range of motion in the thoracic spine.
There are posterior facets on the bodies of the thoracic vertebrae for articulation with the heads of ribs.
T1 has a facet near the top and an inferior facet.
T2 through T9 each have superior and inferior facets.
T10 through T12 each have one facet.
The bodies of the thoracic vertebrae are cylindrical.
The facets of the articular processes in the thoracic region are round and flat.
The articular facets are oriented postero-superolaterally on the superior articular processes and anteroinfero-medially on the inferior processes.
This arrangement permits flexion, extension, and sidebending.
The articular facets are located approximately on the circumference of a circle whose center would be the center of the vertebral body.
This facilitates rotation.
The laminae of the thoracic spine are flat, rectangular, and taller than they are wide.
The laminae of the thoracic spine are stacked on and overlap each other like tiles on a roof.
The transverse processes of the thoracic spine decrease in length from top to bottom.
The transverse processes of T1 through T10 have anterior facets for articulation with the tubercles of ribs.
The spinous processes of the thoracic spine are elongated, laterally compressed, and directed inferiorly (except for T1 and T12).
The characteristics of the thoracic spine help prevent hyperextension.
The vertebral attachment to the rib cage tends to limit mobility of the thoracic spine.
T1 through T7 between the scapulae have corresponding ribs (the 'true ribs') that are connected directly to the sternum by short pieces of cartilage which allow little mobility.
Ribs 8, 9, and 10 (the 'false' ribs) have longer costal cartilages which attach to the cartilage of rib 7 rather than directly to the sternum.
The mobility of T8, T9, and T10 is correspondingly greater.
Ribs 11 and 12 (the 'floating' ribs) have no anterior attachment at all, so the mobility of T11 and T12 is greatest.
The thoracic cage consists of the thoracic vertebrae at the back, and the ribs and sternum in the front.
The sternum is a flat bone located at the front of the thorax.
The sternum consists of three parts: the manubrium, the body, and the xiphoid process.
The top part of the manubrium articulates with the clavicles.
The lateral edge of the body of the sternum has seven notches, which articulate with the first seven costal cartilages.
The xiphoid process articulates with the costal cartilage.
The first rib is the smallest and flattened from top to bottom.

The rib is curved in three different ways: from above, the edge of the rib has the shape of a bucket handle, from the front, it has the shape of an italic S, and when we 'straighten' part of a rib, we can see torsion along its long axis.
The ribs are elongated, flattened, and twisted bones.
Each rib consists of a posterior end with three parts: the head, the neck, and the tubercle; a body; and an anterior end which.
Most ribs articulate with two thoracic vertebrae at three points: the two facets on the head of the rib contact the vertebral bodies, and the tubercle contacts the transverse process.
Exceptions are ribs 1, 11, and 12, which only contact one vertebral body, and ribs 11 and 12, which do not rest on a transverse process.
Each joint is stabilized by numerous small ligaments.
Anteriorly, each rib is attached to the sternum via its costal cartilage, which increases the elasticity of the thoracic cage.
The first seven ribs are short and attach directly to the sternum. These ribs are called true ribs.
The three following cartilages, which are longer, all attach to the 7th rib. These are called false ribs, and have greater mobility.
The two lowest ribs do not have a cartilage. These are called floating ribs.
The movements of a rib can be compared to those of a bucket handle.
As the rib moves up or down, the diameter of the thoracic cage is changed.
Posteriorly, the rib pivots on an axis passing through the center of two joints.
One joint, with two facets, articulates with the vertebral body.
Another joint articulates with the transverse process of the vertebra.
At different levels, the spatial relationship of these two joints changes because the shape of the vertebra changes.
For the superior thoracic vertebrae, the axis is directed more laterally, and elevation of the rib increases the anterior diameter of the thoracic cage.
By contrast, for the inferior thoracic vertebrae, the axis is directed more posteriorly, and elevation of the rib increases the lateral diameter of the thoracic cage.
The anterior ends of the ribs easily accommodate these movements because of the flexibility of the costal cartilages, which varies depending on the level of the rib.
However, this flexibility can decrease with age, thus reducing overall mobility.
During costal inhalation, the ribs are elevated.
Therefore, the diameter of the upper thoracic cage is increased in an anterior direction, while that of the lower cage is increased in a lateral direction.
The reverse occurs during costal exhalation, when the ribs move down.
The costal cartilages undergo some torsion (twisting on their own axis) on inhalation, then return to their normal shape on exhalation.
Obviously, spinal movements also affect the orientation of the ribs.
The ribs in front move closer together during thoracic flexion.
In right sidebending, the ribs move closer together on the right side and farther apart on the left side.
The ribs move farther apart during extension.
In right rotation, the ribs move posteriorly on the right and anteriorly on the left.
The junction between the thoracic and lumbar spinal regions has some interesting features.

T12 resembles T11 in its upper half, but resembles L1 in its lower half, i.e., the spinous process is shortened (which facilitates extension), and the inferior articular facets are large and convex (which restricts rotation).
At T12/L1, the junction between the thoracic and lumbar spinal regions, mobility of the spine is characterized by good flexion, extension, and sidebending, but very little rotation.

Because T11 and T12 are both attached to floating ribs, and because of the shape of the spinous process and inferior articular processes on T11, there is good range of motion in all directions at the T11/T12 joint: flexion, extension, sidebending, and rotation.
Starting from the bottom of the spine, the T11/T12 joint is the first important rotational joint.
Hyper-rotation is possible at this location.
The cervical spine forms the skeleton of the neck.
The cervical spine is divided into two regions: the suboccipital cervical spine and the lower cervical spine.
The suboccipital cervical spine consists of the vertebrae C1 (atlas) and C2 (axis).
C1 (atlas) is located just below the skull.
C1 and C2 have a different shape and function differently from the other vertebrae.
The lower cervical spine consists of vertebrae C3 through C7.
The bodies of cervical vertebrae are small.
The discs are about one-third as thick as the bodies.
The rectangular shape of the cervical vertebral bodies in cross section somewhat restricts sidebending.
The superior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies project upward on the sides (uncinate processes).
The inferior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies are shaped to articulate with the superior ones.
The vertebral bodies are stacked laterally.
The superior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies are slightly convex and tilted forward.
The inferior surfaces of the cervical vertebral bodies are slightly concave and tilted backward.
The lengths of the spinous processes of the cervical vertebrae vary.
The spinous processes in the middle cervical area are short, especially C4, which permits good extension of the cervical spine.
The spinous processes of C6 and C7 are long and restrain extension.
Each cervical transverse process has two roots: one from the side of the body and the other from the pedicle.
The two roots of the cervical transverse process merge to form a medial opening called the transverse foramen and a small lateral groove.
The vertebral artery passes through the transverse foramina of C1-C6.
Spinal nerves pass through the lateral grooves of the cervical transverse processes.
The superior articular facets of the cervical vertebrae face posterosuperiorly, and the inferior facets anteroinferiorly, at an angle of 45°.
Sidebending of the cervical spine is always accompanied by a certain amount of rotation.
The atlas has no vertebral body.
The atlas is essentially a bony ring reinforced by two strong lateral masses.
The atlas has no spinous process.
The transverse ligament of the atlas attaches to the inside of the lateral masses and divides the ring of the atlas into two parts.
The large transverse processes project from the sides of the atlas.
The transverse processes of the atlas contain the transverse foramina, through which the vertebral artery passes.
The anterior part of the transverse processes of the atlas surrounds the dens of the axis.
The posterior part of the transverse processes of the atlas contains the vertebral foramen, through which the spinal cord passes.
The upper and lower surfaces of the lateral masses of the atlas have facets for articulation with the occipital condyle at the top and the axis at the bottom.
The suboccipital spine is the top part of the cervical spine.
The suboccipital spine is the area where movements which are independent of the head are made, like nodding the head 'yes' and shaking it 'no'.
The atlas is the first vertebra at the top of the spine.
The occipital bone is the posteroinferior part of the skull.
On each side of the foramen magnum are the occipital condyles, oval, convex surfaces covered with cartilage.
The corresponding superior articular facets of the atlas are concave.
The articulating surfaces of the occipital condyles and the atlas lie essentially on the outside of an imaginary sphere whose center is inside the skull.
The occipital-atlas joint could be viewed as a ball-and-socket joint, potentially allowing movement in any direction.
The shape of the joint surfaces (longer front-to-back than side-to-side) and presence of ligaments favor flexion/extension and restrict other movements.
There are four ligaments (two lateral, one anterior, one posterior) linking the atlas to the occipital bone.
The same ligaments continue inferiorly onto the axis and help stabilize the axis-atlas joint.
The foramen magnum is a large hole through which the spinal cord passes to merge with the brain.
The axis is the second cervical vertebra.
The axis has the shape of a typical cervical vertebra, except that on the top, there are certain bony particularities which help it to articulate with the atlas.
Each side of the vertebral body of the axis has an oval convex surface, which articulates with a lateral facet of the atlas above.
The vertebral body of the axis has a peg-like process: this is the dens (odontoid process).
The dens fits anteriorly against the anterior arch of C1 and posteriorly against an articular surface of the transverse ligament of C1.
The pivot joint of C1-C2 consists of a ring-like structure covered by cartilage rotating around the dens.
The atlas (C1) presses on the axis (C2) and turns around its pivot.
Rotation is the most important movement at the C1-C2 level, as in shaking your head 'no'.
Rotation of C1 on C2 is accompanied by some lateral gliding of C2 which helps preserve the integrity of the spinal canal.
C1 tends to move slightly closer to C2 during rotation.
There are anterior and posterior ligaments between C2 and C1, as well as ligaments connecting the occiput to the body and dens of C2.
The articular facets of both C1 and C2 are convex.
The back side of the trunk contains numerous muscles, which are arranged in several layers.
The deepest muscles in the back of the trunk attach only to the vertebrae and consist of small bundles that pass from one vertebra to the next.
The intertransverse muscles connect one transverse process to the next, posterior to the intertransverse ligament.
The action of the intertransverse muscles is sidebending.
The interspinalis muscles connect adjacent spinous processes on either side of the ligament.
The action of the interspinalis muscles is extension.
The interspinalis muscles are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C3-S4).
The transversospinalis muscles attach to the back of the vertebrae all along the spine, from sacrum to axis.
The transversospinalis muscles consist of three bundles: the rotatores, the multifidus, and the semispinalis muscles.
The rotatores pass toward the lamina of the vertebra above.
The multifidus pass to the spinous processes of the vertebrae that are located two to four levels above.
The semispinalis muscles pass to the spinous processes of the vertebrae that are located four to six levels above, covering the other layers.
When viewed from behind, the transversospinalis form a 'chevron'-like pattern along the posterior spinal column.
The muscle fibers of the transversospinalis run diagonally from inferior to superior when contracting bilaterally, moving the vertebrae into extension.
The muscle fibers of the transversospinalis run diagonally from medial to lateral, causing sidebending.
The muscle fibers of the transversospinalis run diagonally from anterior to posterior, causing rotation of the spine opposite the side of contraction.
The transversospinalis muscles are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C3-S4).
Muscles located in the convex sagittal curves of the spine elicit a chain reaction.
Electromyographic studies have shown that the activity and importance of the transversospinalis muscles are variable depending upon spinal level.
The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is most important around T6, where the posterior convexity of the thoracic spine is most pronounced.
The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is less significant at T12.
The activity of the transversospinalis muscles is also less significant around L3, where the posterior concavity of the lumbar region is most pronounced.
The actions of the transversospinalis muscles are more predominant where the spine is most convex in the back.
The longus colli muscle is located at the cervical level and helps maintain stability and alignment of the vertebrae and intervertebral discs.
The psoas muscle is located at the lumbar level and helps maintain stability and alignment of the vertebrae and intervertebral discs.

The obliquus capitis superior originates from the transverse process of C1 and inserts on the occiput lateral to rectus capitis posterior major, just posterior to the mastoid process of the temporal bone.
The obliquus capitis inferior runs from the spinous process of C2 to the transverse process of C1.
The obliquus capitis superior, obliquus capitis inferior, and rectus capitis posterior major help produce extension at the C1/C2 joint when they contract bilaterally.
Deep neck muscles are analogous to the transversospinalis but insert on the occiput.
The rectus capitis posterior minor runs from the posterior arch of C1 to the inferior occipital ridge.
The rectus capitis posterior major originates from the spinous process of C2 and inserts just lateral to the minor.
The three muscles that insert on the occiput bend the head to that side by contracting unilaterally.
Obliquus capitis superior, with the most lateral insertion, does this most effectively.
Obliquus capitis superior can produce left rotation by contracting on the right.
These deep muscles have limited lever action because of their small size, but allow great precision of movement.
In cooperation with the anterior neck muscles, they regulate the correct orientation of the head on the neck.
The two rectus capitis muscles
The group of posterior muscles shown on this page forms a layer superficial to those described on the previous pages.
These muscles are sometimes referred to collectively as the sacrospinalis or erector spinae.
There are three muscles in this group: iliocostalis (most lateral), longissimus, and spinalis (most medial; see p. 80).
Each of these is further divided into three subcomponents.
Iliocostalis cervicis runs from the upper six ribs to the transverse processes of the lower cervicals.
Iliocostalis thoracis runs from the lower six to the upper six ribs.
Iliocostalis lumborum originates from the iliac crest via the lumbar fascia (a sheet-like structure made of dense connective tissue), and inserts on the lower ribs.
Longissimus capitis originates from the transverse processes of T3 to C4 and inserts on the mastoid process of the temporal bone.
Longissimus cervicis runs from the transverse processes of the upper thoracic vertebrae to the lower cervical vertebrae.
Longissimus thoracis originates from the lumbar transverse processes and inserts on the thoracic transverse processes and posterior aspect of ribs 9 and 10.
It fills the groove formed where the thoracic vertebrae meet the ribs.
The main action of all these muscles is extension of the spine.
Longissimus capitis contributes to extension of the head (bilateral contraction), or sidebending of the head (unilateral contraction).
By contracting unilaterally, they also contribute to sidebending (especially iliocostalis lumborum, because of its position) and rotation.
Innervation: posterior branches of spinal nerves (C2-L5)
Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis originate from the spinous processes of C7—T1 and the transverse processes of C4—T4, respectively, and insert on the occiput.
If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract bilaterally while the cervical spine is fixed, they extend the head.
If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract unilaterally with the spine fixed, they can contribute to sidebending or rotation.
If spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis contract bilaterally with the head fixed, they tend to straighten out the cervical spine.
Spinalis capitis and semispinalis capitis are innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C1-C5).
Spinalis thoracis runs from the spinous processes of T1 through T10 to the spinous processes of T11 through L2.
Spinalis thoracis extends the spine in the thoracic region.
Spinalis thoracis is innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C2—T10).
The back muscles described thus far (and some later) form a deep muscle layer, also referred to as the spinal muscles.
The spinal muscles have a short lever arm, hence not much power to do certain activities, e.g., extension of the spine from the horizontal position.
The spinal muscles work very precisely.
In the vertical position, the spinal muscles work together to keep the spine straight, rebalancing each other slightly at every vertebral level.
In a standing person, the spinal muscles are almost constantly working.
The spinal muscles are very active muscles, able to work without fatiguing for long stretches of time.
The head 'sits on the neck' for an entire day with the help of the spinal muscles.
Splenius capitis originates from the nuchal ligament and the spinous processes of C7 through T3-T4 and inserts on the mastoid process and adjacent occipital bone.
Splenius capitis is innervated by posterior branches of spinal nerves (C1-C8).
Splenius cervicis runs from the spinous process of T5-T7 to the transverse processes of C1-C3.
Contracting bilaterally, splenius cervicis extends the head and cervical spine.
Contracting unilaterally, splenius cervicis causes sidebending and rotation toward the contracting side.
Levator scapulae runs from the transverse processes of C1-C4 to the medial scapula.
Levator scapulae primarily acts on the scapula; however, when the scapula is fixed, its actions can reinforce those of the splenius cervicis.
Serratus posterior superior runs from the spinous processes of C7 to T3 and inserts on the first five ribs.
Serratus posterior superior elevates the ribs and thereby aids in inspiration.
Serratus posterior superior is innervated by branches of the first four intercostal nerves (T1-T4).
Serratus posterior inferior runs from the spinous processes of T12 to L2 and inserts on the last four ribs.
Serratus posterior inferior depresses these ribs and thereby aids in expiration.
Serratus posterior inferior is innervated by superior branches of the last four intercostal nerves.
Rhomboids, when the scapula is fixed, pull the vertebrae laterally.
Latissimus dorsi, when acting bilaterally, extends the thoracolumbar spine.
Latissimus dorsi, by wrapping around the posteroinferior trunk, helps preserve the structural integrity of the trunk during certain movements.
The trapezius extends the neck when the shoulder bones are fixed and bilaterally contracted.
Unilateral contraction of the superior portion of the trapezius can assist in ipsilateral sidebending or contralateral rotation of the head.
Longus colli is a deep muscle consisting of three portions.
The longitudinal portion of longus colli runs from the bodies of C2 through T3 to the bodies of C4-C7.
The oblique superior portion of longus colli runs from the anterior arch of C1 to the transverse processes of C3-C6.
The oblique inferior portion of longus colli runs from the bodies of T1-T3 to the transverse processes of C5-C7.
Bilateral contraction of longus colli flexes the head and straightens the cervical spine.
Unilaterally, longus colli assists in sidebending and flexion of the head.
Longus colli is innervated by the cervical plexus (C2-C8).
Rectus capitis lateralis runs from the jugular process of the occipital bone to the transverse process of the atlas.
Bilateral contraction of rectus capitis lateralis flexes the head relative to the atlas.
Unilateral contraction of rectus capitis lateralis assists in sidebending.
Rectus capitis anterior runs from the occipital bone to the anterior atlas.
Bilateral contraction of rectus capitis anterior flexes the head relative to the atlas.
Unilateral contraction of rectus capitis anterior assists in sidebending and rotation on the side of the contraction.
Longus capitis originates from the transverse processes of C3-C6 and inserts on the occiput just anterior to the rectus.
Longus colli and longus capitis work together with the scalenes to stabilize the cervical spine, which becomes fixed during inspiration.
The muscles that attach to the cervical spine and the occiput help straighten the upper cervical spine and flex the head (bilateral contraction) or sidebend the head (unilateral contraction).
These muscles are innervated by the cervical plexus (C1-C4).
The scalenes consist of three muscles: Scalenus anterior, Scalenus medius, and Scalenus posterior.
Scalenus anterior and Scalenus medius originate from the transverse processes of C3-C6 and C2-C7 respectively.
Scalenus anterior and Scalenus medius insert close together on the anterior part of rib 1.
Scalenus posterior runs from the transverse processes of C4-C6 to the lateral surface of rib 2.
The scalenes are innervated by the brachial plexus (C4-C8).
The orientation of Scalenus posterior is almost vertical, whereas Scalenus anterior runs obliquely forward.
When the ribs are fixed, unilateral contraction of the scalenes (especially posterior) produces sidebending of the cervical spine.
Medius and anterior also produce some contralateral rotation.
Bilateral contraction of medius and anterior accentuates the curvature of the spine.
When the cervical (and upper thoracic) spine is fixed, contraction of the scalenes elevates ribs 1 and 2, assisting in inspiration.
Longus colli and the scalenes play an important role in stabilizing the cervical spine during this action.
Suprahyoid muscles include hyoglossus, geniohyoid, mylohyoid, digastric, and stylohyoid.
Infrahyoid muscles include sternohyoid, thyrohyoid, and omohyoid.
Among other actions, these muscles assist with the flexion of the head above the neck and thorax.
Sternocleidomastoid has dual origins on the sternum and clavicle, near their junction on the manubrium.
Sternocleidomastoid inserts on the mastoid process and the curved superior occipital line.
Sternocleidomastoid (SCM) is the largest and most important anterior neck muscle.
SCM is innervated by the spinal accessory (11th cranial nerve) and cervical plexus (C1-C2).
When the skull is fixed, SCM elevates the sternum and clavicle, and thereby assists in inspiration.
When the thoracic cage is fixed, unilateral contraction of SCM causes ipsilateral sidebending and contralateral rotation of the head, as well as extension.
Bilateral contraction results in extension of the head, accentuating cervical lordosis.
The intercostal muscles occupy the spaces between adjacent ribs and are arranged in two thin layers.
The fibers of the internal intercostals run downward and backward to the upper border of the rib below.
The external intercostals run downward and forward to the rib below.
The intercostals form a muscular sheet, which joins the ribs to each other, making the thoracic cage a contiguous entity.
The levatores costarum run from the transverse process of a thoracic vertebra to the tubercle of a rib located one or two levels below.
The levatores costarum assist in rotation of the spine or elevation of the ribs, depending on which end is fixed.
The transversus thoracis originates from the posterior surface of the lower sternum and xiphoid process and runs superolaterally to insert on the cartilages of ribs 2 through 6.
The contraction of the transversus thoracis lowers these ribs, assisting in expiration.
The pectoralis major and serratus anterior are described together with the muscles of the shoulder.
The diaphragm is a big flat muscle with 'radiating' fibers located inside the thoracic cage.
The diaphragm extends like a dome between the thoracic and abdominal cavities.
The diaphragm inserts on its own central tendon, which is composed of strong interlacing fibrous tissue and shaped somewhat like a three-leafed clover.
The diaphragm is pierced by three large openings for the inferior vena cava, esophagus, and descending aorta in the middle and near the vertebral column.
The sternal origin of the diaphragm is from the xiphoid process.
The costal origin of the diaphragm is from the deep surfaces of ribs 7-12 and their cartilages; the fibers of attachment interdigitate with those of the transversus abdominis muscle.
The vertebral origin of the diaphragm consists of a 'right crus' arising directly from the bodies of L1-L3, a 'left crus' arising from the bodies of L1-L2, and five arcuate ligaments.
The single median arcuate ligament joins the two crura at the midline and arches over the abdominal entrance of the aorta.
The paired medial arcuate ligaments extend from the body of L1 to its transverse processes, arching over the psoas major muscle.
The paired lateral arcuate ligaments extend from the transverse processes of L1 to rib 12, arching over the quadrates lumborum muscle.
The diaphragm is the principal muscle of inspiration.
The diaphragm is innervated by the phrenic nerves (C3-C5).
The psoas major originates from the sides of the lumbar vertebrae.
The psoas major inserts on the hip.
The psoas major is a multi-articular muscle that passes over eight joints, six of which are intervertebral.
The psoas major can straighten the spine in combination with the posterior transversospinalis muscles.
Bilateral contraction of the psoas major and posterior transversospinalis muscles can erect the lumbar spine.
Unilateral contraction of the psoas major pulls the lumbar spine into sidebending, flexion, and rotation of the side opposite the contraction.
The psoas major is innervated by the lumbar plexus (L1-L3).
The quadratus lumborum originates from the posterior iliac crest and inserts on rib 12 and the transverse processes of L1-L5.
The quadratus lumborum is composed of both vertical and oblique fibers.
Contraction of the quadratus lumborum pulls on rib 12 and the other ribs, causing sidebending of the lumbar spine and ribcage.
The quadratus lumborum is an expiratory muscle.
When the ribs and spine are fixed, the quadratus lumborum raises the pelvis on one side.
The quadratus lumborum is innervated by the lumbar plexus (T12/L1-L3).
There are four paired muscles in the abdominal wall.
The transversus abdominis is the deepest of the four abdominal muscles.

The transversus abdominis attaches below to the inguinal ligament and the iliac crest, posteriorly to the five lumbar vertebrae, above to the inner surfaces of the last seven ribs, and anteriorly to the linea alba.
Contraction of the transversus abdominis reduces the diameter of the abdomen.
When the vertebrae are fixed, the transversus abdominis pulls in the belly.
When the anterior aponeurosis of the transversus abdominis is fixed, it increases lordosis of the lumbar spine.
The transversus abdominis is innervated by the intercostal nerves (T7-T12), ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
The internal oblique muscle lies between the transversus and external oblique muscles.
The internal oblique muscle is attached below to the inguinal ligament and the iliac crest.
The internal oblique muscle is attached posteriorly to the lumbodorsal fascia.
The internal oblique muscle is attached above to the lower four ribs.
The internal oblique muscle is attached anteriorly to a very broad aponeurosis.
The fibers of the internal oblique muscle run in various directions depending on their location.
The average direction of the internal oblique muscle fibers is anterosuperior.
Unilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle results in sidebending or ipsilateral rotation of the spine and ribcage.
If the pelvis is fixed, unilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle acts on the ribs.
If the ribs are fixed, unilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle acts on the pelvis.
Bilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle causes compression of the abdomen and assists in flexion of the trunk when the pelvis is fixed.
When the vertebrae and pelvis are fixed, bilateral contraction of the internal oblique muscle lowers the ribs and moves them backward.
The internal oblique muscle is involved in expiration.
The internal oblique muscle is innervated by the intercostal nerves (T9-T12), ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
The external oblique muscle is attached above to the outer surfaces of ribs 5-12.
The fibers of the external oblique muscle intertwine with those of the serratus anterior.
The external oblique muscle is attached to the ilioinguinal ligament.
The external oblique muscle forms a broad aponeurosis in front and below, ending at and contributing to the linea alba and inguinal ligament.
The average direction of the external oblique muscle fibers is anteroinferior.
Unilateral contraction of the external oblique muscle results in side-bending and contralateral rotation of the spine and ribcage.
Bilateral contraction of the external oblique muscle causes flexion of the trunk.
The oblique muscles work synergistically in rotation of the trunk.
Rotation of the trunk to the left involves simultaneous contraction of the left internal oblique and right external oblique.
The external oblique muscle is innervated by the intercostal nerves (T5-T12) and ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
When the pelvis is fixed, the external oblique muscle lowers the ribs and is then an expiratory muscle.
The rectus abdominis muscle is located anteriorly inside a 'rectus sheath' formed by the aponeuroses of the three preceding muscles.
The rectus abdominis muscle runs from the crest and symphysis of the pubis to the xiphoid process and cartilages of ribs 5-7.
The rectus abdominis muscle adheres to the anterior layer of its sheath at three tendinous intersections.
The tendinous intersections are visible as transverse grooves when the muscle contracts.
The rectus abdominis muscle is the most direct flexor of the trunk by moving the sternum toward the pelvis.
The rectus abdominis muscle can also flex the trunk by tilting the pelvis backward.
The rectus abdominis muscle is innervated by the last four intercostal nerves (T5-T12) and ilioinguinal and iliohypogastric nerves (L1).
Levator ani originates from fascia covering the obturator internus, along a line extending from the posterior body of the pubis to the ischial spine.
The fibers from the left and right sides of the levator ani muscle run inferomedially and meet each other at the midline.
Some posterior fibers of the levator ani muscle insert on the coccyx and lower sacrum.
Levator ani contains an opening for the anal canal.
The anterior portion of the levator ani muscle is different in the female pelvis, where it has an opening to accommodate the vulva.
In the male pelvis, the anterior portion of the levator ani muscle is closed.
Innervation of the levator ani muscle is the accessory branch of the sacral plexus (S3).
Coccygeus is posterior to levator ani, running from the ischial spine to the coccyx and lower sacrum.
Both levator ani and coccygeus muscles support the weight of the pelvic organs and are involved in continence.
Levator ani and coccygeus muscles rotate the sacrum backward.
Innervation of the coccygeus muscle is the accessory branch of the pudendal plexus (S4).
Levator ani and coccygeus muscles do not play a role in the positioning of the pelvis on top of the legs since they do not insert into the femurs.
The pelvic diaphragm forms the floor of the pelvis and consists of two paired muscles: levator ani and coccygeus.
Inferior to the pelvic diaphragm and overlapping it anteriorly is the urogenital diaphragm.
The abdominal cavity is inferior to the thorax and contains the abdominal organs.
The abdominal cavity is bounded above by the diaphragm, lower ribs, costal cartilages, and sternum.
The abdominal cavity is bounded posteriorly by the lumbar vertebrae.
The abdominal cavity is bounded laterally and anteriorly by the abdominal muscles.
The abdominal cavity is bounded inferiorly by the pelvis and the pelvic and urogenital diaphragm.
The abdomen can be compared to a liquid-filled, flexible container which can change its shape but not its volume (i.e., is non-compressible).
The thorax can be compared to a gas-filled container which can change its shape and is compressible.

The diaphragm is like a plunger that moves between the thorax and abdomen and helps control the shape, volume, and pressure of both cavities during activities such as breathing, speaking, coughing, defecating, childbirth, and hiccups.
During normal inspiration, contraction of the diaphragm lowers its dome and increases the volume of the thorax and lungs.
The increase in volume during inspiration results in a decrease in pressure inside the lungs, which causes air to enter from the outside environment.
Contraction of the abdominal muscles can cause the abdomen to maintain its shape and resist lowering of the diaphragm.
In the case of abdominal muscle contraction, the central tendon becomes the fixed point and contraction of the fibers of the diaphragm results in elevation of the ribs.

The elevation of the ribs during contraction of the diaphragm is due to the superomedial orientation of the fibers and lateral pressure from the contents of the abdomen, which are being compressed from above.
During normal expiration, the diaphragm simply relaxes (i.e., the dome moves upward) and the elastic lung tissue returns to its normal size after being stretched during inspiration.
The decrease in lung volume during expiration results in an increase in pressure, and air is expelled through the nose or mouth.
The lungs do not empty completely during expiration.

In 'forced' (or active) expiration, the expiratory muscles, especially the abdominal muscles, contract and press the abdominal organs upward against the thorax and lower ribcage, further decreasing lung volume and increasing the pressure which drives air out.
No matter how hard 'forced' expiration is done, some air will always remain in the lungs. This is called residual volume.
The shoulder is the area where the arm is attached to the thorax.
The shoulder involves more than one joint in contrast to the hip.

The shoulder has two important functions: flexibility to allow the hand and arm a large range of motion and providing a strong, stable fixed point for actions like lifting heavy objects or pushing against resistance.
The primary joint of the shoulder is the glenohumeral joint between the head of the humerus and the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
The scapula is an extremely mobile bone and is connected to the axial skeleton (sternum) only by the long, thin clavicle.

Two other joints involved in shoulder movements are the acromioclavicular joint between the distal clavicle and the acromion process of the scapula and the sternoclavicular joint between the medial clavicle and the manubrium of the sternum.
