The shoulder comprises three joints plus important gliding planes.
We can distinguish functional areas of the shoulder: the scapulothoracic and the scapulohumeral.
There are two types of shoulder movements: movements of the shoulder on the thorax and combined actions of the scapula and arm.
Shoulder movements on the thorax include elevation, depression, abduction, adduction, lateral rotation, and medial rotation.
Elevation is the movement of the shoulder upward.
Depression is the movement of the shoulder downward.
Abduction is the movement of the shoulder away from the spine.
Adduction is the movement of the shoulder closer to the spine.
Lateral rotation is the tilt of the shoulder by moving the inferior angle away from the midline.
Medial rotation is the tilt of the shoulder by moving the inferior angle of the scapula toward the midline.
The combined actions of the scapula and arm allow the arm to move anteriorly (flexion), posteriorly (extension), laterally (abduction), and medially (adduction).
Anteriorly (flexion) is the movement of the arm beyond 90 degrees.
Posteriorly (extension) is the movement of the arm, with a smaller range than flexion.
Laterally (abduction) is the movement of the arm beyond 90 degrees, closer to the midline.
Medially (adduction) is the movement of the arm closer to the body.
Adduction is combined with extension to move the arm behind the body or with flexion to move the arm in front of the body.
Rotation of the humerus (on its axis) is best visualized with the elbow bent to avoid confusion with pronation and supination of the forearm.
Medial rotation is the rotation of the humerus inward.
Lateral rotation is the rotation of the humerus outward.
At their extremes, arm movements affect the thoracic spine and ribcage.
Flexion of the arms is associated with extension of the spine and 'opening' of the anterior ribcage (i.e., ribs move apart from each other).
Extension of the arms causes flexion of the spine and 'closing' of the ribcage.
Adduction is associated with ipsilateral sidebending and closing of the ipsilateral hemithorax.
Abduction causes contralateral sidebending and opening of the ipsilateral hemithorax.
Medial or lateral rotation of the humerus is associated with similar rotation of the spine.
The shoulder girdle consists of the scapulae (posteriorly) and the clavicles and sternum (anteriorly).
The clavicle (collarbone) is a flattened, elongated bone with two bends in it, so that it is roughly S-shaped when viewed from above or below.
The clavicle articulates medially with the manubrium of the sternum.
Laterally, the clavicle articulates with the acromion of the scapula.
The medial end of the clavicle is roughly triangular with a rounded articular surface which is concave transversely and convex vertically (saddle-shaped).
The medial end of the clavicle meets an inversely-shaped articular surface on the manubrium.
The clavicle can move upward (elevation), rotate around its longitudinal axis, move backward (extension), move forward (flexion), and move downward (depression).
These movements generally occur secondarily in association with movements of the scapula.
The scapula, or shoulderblade, is a flat triangular bone with two surfaces (anterior and posterior), three borders, and three angles.
The lateral border of the scapula is much thicker than the medial border.
The lateral angle of the scapula contains the glenoid cavity, a shallow oval-shaped depression which articulates with the head of the humerus.
The coracoid process arises from the superior border medial to the glenoid cavity and looks like a bent finger pointing forward.
The anterior surface of the scapula is smooth and slightly concave and leans in a mobile fashion against the ribcage.
The posterior surface of the scapula is convex.
The spine of the scapula is a strong, sharp ridge running diagonally near the superior border.
There are depressions above and below the spine called the supraspinous and infraspinous fossae.
The scapular spine is a triangular ridge, which starts roughly perpendicular to the scapular plane.
The posterior side of the spine is thick, tilting in two directions.
The inferior portion of the spine has an enlarged tubercle for attachment of the trapezius.
The acromion and the lateral end of the clavicle each has a small oval surface where they articulate.
Sometimes the acromioclavicular joint includes a meniscus (fibrous disk).
The shape of the articular surfaces allows some gliding movement, as well as opening and closing of the angle formed by the two bones.

The capsule of the acromioclavicular joint is loose and the joint is supported by four ligaments: the superior and inferior acromioclavicular ligaments, which inhibit the opening of the angle between the two bones; and the conoid and trapezoid ligaments, which inhibit the closing of the angle.
These two ligaments are stretched between the coracoid process and the clavicle.
The lateral end of the spine is enlarged and flattened to form the acromion process.
Note that the acromion is oriented perpendicular to the spine.
The posterior surface of the acromion process can easily be palpated through the skin.
The anterior surface of the acromion process projects beyond the glenoid cavity; its anterior side consists of an oval articular surface, which articulates with the lateral edge of the clavicle.
The mobility of the sternoclavicular and acromioclavicular joints allows the scapula to move in various directions.
In elevation, the scapula moves upward and away from the ribcage.
In depression, the scapula moves downward and fits more snugly against the ribcage.
In retraction (adduction), the medial border of the scapula moves closer to the vertebral column.
In protraction (abduction), the medial border of the scapula moves away from the vertebral column, and the scapula moves anteriorly at a 45° angle as it glides on the convex thorax.
To understand rotation, visualize a mobile scapula positioned above the ribcage and moving around an axis, which is vertical to the ribcage and passes under the middle of the scapular spine.
The scapula pivots around this axis.
The glenoid cavity of the scapula can point in many directions.
This greatly increases the range of motion of the glenohumeral joint.
Mobility of the arm is greatly increased by such movements of the scapula.
The free movement of the scapula is aided by the presence of some fatty layers ('gliding planes').
One is located between the serratus anterior muscle and the ribcage.
Another is located between the subscapularis and the serratus anterior muscles.
This is the long bone of the upper arm.

Landmarks of the proximal end of the humerus include the lateral greater tubercle, the anterior lesser tubercle for muscle attachment, and the bicipital (intertubercular) groove running between the two tubercles.
The shaft itself, in cross section, is circular near the proximal end but becomes more triangular (with a pronounced anterior ridge) near the distal end.
The triangular shape of the humerus here allows us to distinguish three surfaces and three ridges.
The medial head whose spheroid surface articulates with the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
The anatomical neck is just below the head.
The distal end is enlarged and forms articulating surfaces for the bones of the forearm at the elbow joint.
The glenohumeral joint unites the head of the humerus with the glenoid fossa of the scapula.
The articular surfaces of the glenohumeral joint consist of the head of the humerus and the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
The head of the humerus is oriented mostly medial, but also slightly posterosuperior.
The surface area of the head of the humerus is two or three times larger than that of the glenoid cavity.
The glenoid cavity is fairly shallow.
The glenoid labrum is a fibrocartilaginous ring that attaches to the glenoid cavity and increases the fit of the shoulder joint surfaces.
The glenoid cavity is oval and slightly concave anteriorly.
The glenoid cavity is oriented mostly lateral, but also slightly anterosuperior.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches to the scapula on the outer rim of the glenoid cavity.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches anterosuperiorly to the coracoid process.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint encircles the origin of the long head of the biceps.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint attaches around the head of the humerus.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint has many folds, especially in its inferior portion.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint gives it a good range of motion for anterior flexion and abduction.
The capsule of the glenohumeral joint is reinforced at the top and front by ligaments.
The coracohumeral ligament runs from the coracoid process and forms two bundles that run to the greater and lesser tubercle of the humerus.
The coracohumeral ligament is the strongest ligament of this joint.
Ligaments run from the border of the glenoid cavity to the anatomical neck of the humerus.
The ligaments consist of three bundles: superior, middle, and inferior.
There are weak areas between the ligaments.
The capsuloligamentary structure of the shoulder is not very strong.
The glenohumeral part of the shoulder is stabilized especially by the deepest muscles, which form a cuff of 'active ligaments' around the shoulder, called the rotator cuff.
The coracoacromial ligament stretches across the scapula and attaches to the acromion and the coracoid process.
The coracoacromial ligament protects the tendon of the supraspinatus.
If the humerus is lifted too much, the coracoacromial ligament can rub against the tendon and can paradoxically cause it to wear out.
The resting position of the joint is where the arm is in slight flexion, abduction, and internal rotation.
The serratus anterior muscle is a broad, thin muscle covering the lateral ribcage.
The serratus anterior muscle originates from the upper ten ribs and inserts along the entire medial border of the scapula.
When the ribs are fixed, the serratus anterior muscle flattens the medial border of the scapula against the ribcage.
With the upper fibers, the serratus anterior muscle pulls the scapula laterally (abduction) and into upward rotation.
The serratus anterior muscle is innervated by the long thoracic nerve (C5-C7).
The fibers of the serratus anterior muscle are contracted and visible when the arm is pushing against some resistance.
In actions such as push-ups, where great force is being exerted by the arms, the serratus anterior muscle keeps the scapula fixed in place and tight against the ribcage.
In situations where great force is being exerted by the arms, the middle fibers of trapezius (an adductor) and serratus (an abductor) contract simultaneously to stabilize the scapula.
There are some fatty layers ('gliding planes') separating serratus from the ribcage and from the subscapularis muscle.
These fatty layers increase the mobility of the scapula and are important in many complex movements of the shoulder.
If the scapula is fixed, the lower fibers of serratus anterior lift the middle ribs, acting as inspiratory muscles.
The subclavius muscle runs from rib 1 and its cartilage to the underside of the clavicle.
The pectoralis minor originates from ribs 3-5 and inserts on the coracoid process.
When the ribs are fixed, the pectoralis minor muscle pulls the scapula downward and forward, as if to tilt the scapula above the ribcage by lifting the inferior angle upward.
When the scapula is fixed, the pectoralis minor muscle assists in inspiration by elevating the ribs.
The pectoralis minor muscle is innervated by the medial pectoral nerve (C7-T1).
The sternocleidomastoid (SCM) acts primarily on the head and cervical spine.
If the head is fixed, the SCM can elevate the area where the clavicle and sternum meet, and thereby assist in inspiration.
The action of the sternocleidomastoid is to depress the clavicle.
The levator scapulae originates from the transverse processes of C1-C4 and inserts on the superior angle of the scapula.
The rhomboids (major and minor) originate from the spinous processes of C7 and T1-T4 and insert on the medial border of the scapula.
The actions of the levator scapulae are to elevate the scapula and rotate it downward; when the scapula is fixed, it acts on the cervical spine.
The actions of the rhomboids are to adduct the scapula and rotate it downward; when the scapula is fixed, they act on the thoracic spine.
The levator scapulae, rhomboids, and upper trapezius elevate the scapula.
The lower trapezius and lower serratus anterior depress the scapula.
The serratus anterior abducts the scapula.
The trapezius and rhomboids adduct the scapula.
The serratus anterior, upper trapezius, and lower trapezius upward rotate the scapula.
The rhomboids and levator scapulae downward rotate the scapula.
The orientation of trapezius fibers ranges from inferolateral to superolateral.
Simultaneous contraction of all trapezius fibers adducts the scapula.
The upper fibers of the trapezius act in elevation and upward rotation of the scapula.
The lower fibers of the trapezius act in depression and upward rotation of the scapula.
The trapezius originates on the occiput, nuchal ligament, and spinous processes of the cervical vertebrae and thoracic vertebrae down to T12.
The trapezius inserts on the lateral third of the clavicle and acromion (upper fibers), scapular spine (middle fibers), and a tubercle at the medial end of the scapular spine (lower fibers).
The subscapularis originates from the anterior surface of the scapula and inserts on the lesser tubercle of the humerus.
The subscapularis fibers converge at the lateral angle of the scapula.
The subscapularis is the principal muscle of medial rotation of the arm.
The subscapularis is innervated by the upper subscapular nerve (C5-C6).
The supraspinatus originates from the supraspinous fossa on the posterior scapula.
The supraspinatus tendon passes under the acromioclavicular joint and the ligament connecting the coracoid process to the acromion.
The supraspinatus inserts on the highest point on the greater tubercle.
A large bursa surrounds the supraspinatus tendon, separating it from the inferior surface of the acromion and deltoid muscle.
The bursa surrounding the supraspinatus tendon acts as an auxiliary component of the glenohumeral joint.
Adhesions around the supraspinatus bursa can restrict shoulder mobility.
The supraspinatus abducts the arm; its action is weak but coupled with that of the deltoid.
The supraspinatus is innervated by the subscapular nerve (C5-C6).
The infraspinatus originates from the infraspinous fossa.
The infraspinatus tendon passes above the capsule of the glenohumeral joint and inserts on the greater tubercle.
The infraspinatus insertion point is posteroinferior to the insertion of the supraspinatus.
The infraspinatus actions are lateral rotation and participation in abduction.
The infraspinatus is innervated by the suprascapular nerve (C4-C6).
The teres minor originates from the lateral border of the scapula (posterior surface) and inserts on the greater tubercle below the insertion of the infraspinatus.
The teres minor action is lateral rotation.
The teres minor is innervated by the axillary nerve (C5-C6).
The rotator cuff muscles are composed of the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor.
The rotator cuff muscles tendons surround and reinforce the shoulder-joint capsule on three sides.
The rotator cuff muscles mobilize the humerus and act as 'active ligaments' providing mobility to the joint.
The supraspinatus prevents the humerus from being dislocated/displaced upward.
The subscapularis prevents the head of the humerus from gliding backward, forward, and backward.
The infraspinatus and teres minor prevent the humerus from being dislocated forward.
The shoulder is a joint whose bony structure and passive attachments (capsule, ligaments) are only weak stabilizing structures.
The main stabilizing force of the shoulder comes from the action of the periarticular muscles.
The action of the periarticular muscles can be upset, which is why the shoulder is often a problem area causing joint pain.
The coracobrachialis muscle arises from the coracoid process and inserts on the medial surface of the humeral shaft, near the middle.
The action of the coracobrachialis muscle is to flex and adduct the arm.
The coracobrachialis muscle is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve (C6-C7).
The biceps brachii muscle participates in flexion of the shoulder.
The long head of the biceps brachii muscle is involved in abduction of the arm.
The short head of the biceps brachii muscle is involved in adduction of the arm.
The triceps brachii muscle participates in adduction of the arm.
The pectoralis major muscle has a clavicular head that originates from the anterior, medial clavicle and a sternocostal head that originates from the sternum and costal cartilages 1-6 and rib 7.
The tendon of the pectoralis major muscle is twisted such that the fibers from the clavicular head insert below those from the sternocostal head on the lateral aspect of the bicipital groove.
When the ribcage is fixed, all the fibers of the pectoralis major muscle adduct and medially rotate the arm.
The superior fibers of the pectoralis major muscle are involved in flexion up to 60°, then the inferior fibers take over and continue flexion up to 0°.
When the shoulder is fixed, the superior fibers of the pectoralis major muscle lower the clavicle and the inferior fibers participate in inspiration.
When the shoulder is fixed while the arm is flexed, all the fibers of the pectoralis major muscle are involved in inspiration stretching of pectoralis major.
The pectoralis major muscle is innervated by the lateral and medial pectoral nerves (C5-T1).
The latissimus dorsi muscle originates from the sacral and iliac crests, thoracolumbar fascia, spinous processes of T7-T12, and posterior surfaces of the four lower ribs.
The tendon of the latissimus dorsi muscle wraps around the medial side of the humerus, makes a twist, and inserts on the bicipital groove.
The actions of the latissimus dorsi muscle are extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the arm.
If the humerus is fixed, the actions of the latissimus dorsi muscle are as described on page 82.
The latissimus dorsi muscle is innervated by the thoracodorsal nerve (C6-C8).
The teres major muscle originates from the posterior surface of the inferior angle of the scapula and inserts on the medial aspect of the bicipital groove, next to the latissimus dorsi muscle.
The actions of the teres major muscle are the same as those of the latissimus dorsi muscle, but much less powerful.
The deltoid muscle is superficial and gives the shoulder its characteristic shape.
The deltoid muscle consists of three groups of fibers: middle, posterior, and anterior.
The middle fibers of the deltoid muscle attach to the lateral border of the acromion.
The posterior fibers of the deltoid muscle attach to the spine of the scapula (inferior portion of the posterior border).
The anterior fibers of the deltoid muscle attach to the clavicles (lateral third of the anterior scapular border).
The three groups of deltoid fibers converge toward the middle of the arm and insert on the lateral surface of the humerus.
Contraction of the anterior deltoid muscle fibers causes flexion and medial rotation of the arm.
Contraction of the middle deltoid muscle fibers causes abduction of the arm.
Contraction of the posterior deltoid muscle fibers causes extension of the arm.
The deltoid muscle is innervated by the axillary nerve (C5-C6).
Muscle actions in shoulder flexion include: anterior deltoid, pectoralis major, and coracobrachialis. Accessory muscles include biceps brachii and subscapularis.

Muscle actions in shoulder extension include: posterior deltoid, latissimus dorsi, and teres major. Accessory muscles include teres minor, short head of biceps, long head of triceps, and coracobrachialis.
Muscle actions in shoulder abduction include: deltoid and supraspinatus. Accessory muscles include infraspinatus and long head of biceps.
Muscle actions in shoulder lateral rotation include: infraspinatus, teres minor, and posterior deltoid.
Muscle actions in shoulder adduction include: latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, and teres major.
The subscapularis, latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, teres major, and anterior deltoid muscles are involved in medial rotation of the shoulder.
Muscle actions in the shoulder change depending on the arm's position.
Adductor and medial rotator muscles are predominant in shoulder muscle actions.
The pectoralis major muscle is a flexor of the arm up to 60 degrees.
Beyond 90 degrees, the pectoralis major muscle functions as an extensor, bringing the arm back toward anatomical position.
The elbow joint allows the upper limb to fold on itself and extend.
The elbow joint enables flexion and extension actions, allowing the distance between the shoulder and hand to be shortened or lengthened.
The elbow joint participates in rotating the forearm around its longitudinal axis.
The elbow joint's pronation-supination action multiplies the possible positions of the hand.
Flexion of the elbow joint decreases the angle between the anterior surfaces of the arm and forearm.
In active elbow flexion, the movement is limited by contact between the bodies of the muscles involved.
In passive elbow flexion, the angle may become slightly smaller since the muscles are more compressible.
Extension of the elbow is a return from flexion to anatomical position, which is an increase in the angle between the arm and forearm.
Generally, an increase of the elbow angle past 180° is impossible due to contact between the olecranon process and its fossa.
In a few exceptional individuals, the elbow angle can become slightly larger than 180°; this is called 'recurvation'.
The forearm consists of two long bones: the radius and the ulna.
Each of the radius and ulna has a shaft and two extremities.
The radius and ulna are triangular shaped and therefore have three surfaces and three edges.
The proximal part of the radius is small, while its distal part is large.
The proximal extremity of the radius consists of two parts: the head, which is covered by cartilage, and the neck.
The head of the radius consists of a top part (beveled medially) and a circumference.
At its distal part, the medial edge of the radius bifurcates, so that the cross section of the bone is quadrangular.
The inferior surface of the radius articulates with the wrist.
The bifurcation of the radius has a concave articular surface which articulates with the ulna, which is the ulnar notch of the radius.
There is another protuberance at the lateral distal end of the radius: the styloid process.
The shaft of the radius has a cylindrical shape with three surfaces and three edges.
The proximal part of the ulna is large, while its distal part is small.
The proximal part of the ulna has two large processes: the olecranon and the coronoid process.
The distal extremity of the ulna is called the ulnar head.
Laterally, the ulnar head has a convex articular surface which articulates with the radius.
Medially, the ulnar head has a protuberance, the styloid process.
Attached to the inferior surface of the ulna is a triangular ligament, which connects the ulna to the bones of the wrist.
Hollow areas, where the bone becomes tapered, are common on the articular surfaces.
Just above the trochlear eminence is the coronoid fossa anteriorly and the olecranon fossa posteriorly.
Just above the capitulum is the radial fossa of the humerus.
At the distal end of the humerus, the anterior edge bifurcates and the bone flattens out, becomes larger, and curves forward.
The humerus has two lateral bony projections, the medial and lateral epicondyles.
Posteriorly, there is an olecranon fossa (not shown).
The two protuberances (medial and lateral epicondyles) delimit a triangular space.

At the base of this triangle, there are two articular surfaces: the medial pulley-shaped surface is oblique medioinferiorly and articulates with the trochlear notch of the ulna. This is the trochlea of the humerus.
The lateral rounded surface with a diameter of about 1cm is the capitulum.
The surfaces between the shafts of the radius and ulna are connected via a thin layer: the interosseous membrane.
At the head of the radius, on its upper surface, is a shallow cup or fovea which articulates with the capitulum.
The radius is lined by a beveled layer, which articulates with the interosseous membrane.
The anterior surface of the olecranon and the superior surface of the coronoid process form an almost continuous surface in the shape of a hollow cylinder.
This surface is the trochlear notch of the ulna.
The trochlear notch of the ulna is covered with cartilage and separated into two grooves by a longitudinal ridge.
The trochlear notch articulates with the trochlea of the humerus.
From an anatomical point of view, the radial notch of the ulna is not part of the elbow joint because it is not contained in the same capsule.
The olecranon consists of five surfaces, including a beak-like projection on its superior surface.
The coronoid process has four surfaces, with a beak-like projection of its own on its anterior surface.
Three bones—the humerus, ulna, and radius—come together to form the capsule of the elbow joint.
The capsule attaches to the circumference of the coronoid and olecranon fossae of the humerus and connects with the medial and lateral epicondyles without encircling them.
The capsule attaches to the circumference of the neck and the radius.
The capsule also attaches to the circumference of the trochlear notch of the ulna.
The capsule is taut in front (especially laterally), but loose in back (facilitating flexion).
The anterior and posterior elbow ligaments are minor ligaments.
Posteriorly, the fibers of the anterior and posterior elbow ligaments cross over.
Anteriorly, they are fan-shaped and reinforce the capsule.
The collateral ligaments are the most important ligaments of the elbow.
The ulnar collateral ligament arises from the medial epicondyle and attaches below to the medial coronoid process and olecranon.
The radial collateral ligament is formed by three fasciae that arise from the lateral epicondyle.
Two slips of the radial collateral ligament encircle the head of the radius, one anteriorly and the other posteriorly, before inserting at the radial notch of the ulna.
The third slip of the radial collateral ligament inserts on the lateral olecranon.
Both collateral ligaments prevent any lateral movement of the elbow joint.
The surfaces of the lower part of the humerus articulate with the ulna and radius.
This functional unit facilitates movement only in the sagittal plane.
The head of the radius fits into the radial fossa of the humerus.
The 'beak' of the coronoid process fits into the coronoid fossa.
In extension, the olecranon process of the ulna fits against its fossa on the humerus.
The axis of the trochlea is directed obliquely superolaterally; the medial side is lower than the lateral side.
For this reason, the axes of the humerus and ulna are not quite parallel when the elbow is extended, i.e., the edges of the arm and forearm form an angle that is more or less pronounced.
During flexion, the anterior concave shape of the bones allows them to accommodate the bulk of the muscle mass.
Two muscle groups are presented: the principal muscles (in boldface) and the secondary muscles, most of which will be described in Chapter 5 on the wrist and hand.
The biceps brachii has two origins: the long head and the short head.
The long head of the biceps brachii arises from a tubercle above the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
The long head of the biceps brachii travels through the shoulder joint, between the greater and lesser tubercles, and along the bicipital groove before merging with the body.
The two heads of the biceps brachii then continue downward and form one tendon, which passes anterior to the elbow joint and inserts at the bicipital tuberosity of the radius.

The short head of the biceps brachii starts as a tendon at the coracoid process on the lateral edge of the scapula and becomes a fleshy body which joins with the muscle fibers of the long head about halfway down the humerus.
The biceps brachii is the primary elbow flexor.
The biceps brachii also supinates the radius at the elbow.
The brachialis arises from the anterior surface of the distal humerus and inserts on the coronoid process.
The brachialis is a major flexor of the elbow.
The brachialis is innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve (C5-C6).
The brachioradialis originates from a lateral ridge on the distal humerus and inserts via a long tendon on the base of the radial styloid process.
The brachioradialis flexes the elbow.
In pronation or supination, the brachioradialis brings the forearm into a middle position.
The contraction of the brachioradialis can be seen along the radius when the elbow is flexed and the forearm is pronated or supinated.
The anconeus muscle runs from the lateral epicondyle of the humerus to the superior ulna.
The action of the anconeus muscle is to extend the elbow and it plays a small role as an abductor during pronation of the ulna.
The triceps brachii muscle consists of three heads: the long head, the lateral head, and the medial head (also called the deep head).
The long head of the triceps brachii originates from a tubercle below the glenoid cavity of the scapula.
The lateral head of the triceps brachii originates from the lateral posterosuperior shaft of the humerus.
The medial head of the triceps brachii originates from the posteroinferior humerus, where it is covered by the body.
The triceps brachii has a single broad insertion by a tendon onto the olecranon.
The triceps brachii is the major elbow extensor.
The long head of the triceps brachii also participates in extension of the arm due to its attachment to the scapula.
The triceps brachii and the anconeus muscle are innervated by the radial nerve (C7-C8).
Pronation of the elbow involves the radius crossing over the ulna, causing the palm to face posteriorly and the thumb to face inward.
Supination of the elbow involves the radius and ulna becoming parallel, causing the palm to face anteriorly and the thumb to face outward.
Pronation and supination of the elbow are possible due to the interplay of articular surfaces and ligaments at the proximal and distal ends of the forearm.
The ulna has a concave radial notch located lateral to the coronoid process.
The radial notch and ligament are lined with synovial membrane and form a ringlike structure around the radial head.
The annular (or transverse carpal) ligament attaches at the front and back of the radial notch.
The quadrate ligament connects the radial notch of the ulna to the neck of the radius.
Below the radial head is an area that articulates with the capitulum of the humerus.
The ring formed by the radial notch, ligament, and quadrate ligament allows easy rotation of the radial head during pronation and supination.
The ring is somewhat funnel-shaped, which helps resist downward movement of the radius when the forearm is supporting a heavy weight.
The ulnar notch is located where the medial edge of the radius bifurcates.
The distal radioulnar joint consists of the convex ulnar head fitting into the concave ulnar notch of the radius.
The articular disc runs from the styloid process of the ulna to the ulnar notch of the radius.
The anterior and posterior edges of the articular disc are thick, which gives it a concave shape.
Both surfaces of the articular disc are covered by cartilage.
The articular disc is both an articular surface with the inferior surface of the ulnar head and therefore with the wrist and a connecting structure.
During pronation, the posterior portion of the articular disc becomes taut; during supination, the anterior portion becomes taut.
The interosseous membrane connects the shafts of the radius and ulna.
The interosseous membrane has diagonal fibers oriented in both directions.
Pronation involves a conical movement of the radius around the ulna.
The proximal end of the radius pivots on itself, but with a slight 'give' due to the suppleness of the annular ligament.
The distal end of the radius glides anteromedially around the head of the ulna.
In the first type of pronation (e.g., turning a key), the axis for movement of the hand passes through the middle finger, and the ulna moves slightly in conjunction with the radius.
Anconeus is involved in the first type of pronation.
In the second type of pronation (e.g., flipping the page of a book), the axis of the hand passes through the fifth finger, and the ulna remains fixed.
The ulna and radius, in anatomical position, are both concave anteriorly.
The curvature of the ulna and radius allows the radius to cross over the ulna during pronation.
If both bones were straight, they would contact each other too soon and normal pronation would be impossible.
Fractures or other injuries can alter these curvatures and thereby interfere with pronation.
The pronator teres arises from two fasciae on the medial epicondyle of the humerus and coronoid process of the ulna and inserts on the midlateral surface of the radius.
The pronator teres is the major pronator of the forearm and assists in flexion of the elbow.
The pronator teres is innervated by the median nerve (C6-C7).
The pronator quadratus is a square-shaped muscle running between the anterior surfaces of the distal ulna and radius.
The pronator quadratus pulls the radius across the ulna in pronation.
The pronator quadratus is innervated by the anterior interosseous nerve (C8-T1).
Supinators are attached to four bones.
The humerus is one of the four bones that supinators are attached to.
The radius is one of the four bones that supinators are attached to.
The biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator.
The biceps brachii acts by 'unfurling' the proximal part of the radius.
The brachioradialis acts as a supinator only when it is in a pronated position.
The brachioradialis moves the forearm into position halfway between pronation and supination.
The supinator has two layers: a deep one and a superficial one.
The deep layer of the supinator originates from the superolateral part of the ulna.
The superficial layer of the supinator originates from the lateral epicondyle of the humerus.
The supinator muscle wraps around the radius.
The supinator inserts between the neck (deep fibers) and the lateral side of the bone (superficial fibers).
The radius has a 'supinating curve' with the biceps brachii and supinator inserting near the top.
The supinator action is to supinate the forearm.
The supinator is innervated by the deep radial nerve (C5-C6).
The hand is a versatile tool due to the enormous mobility of the fingers and the arrangement of the thumb.
The opposable thumbs makes it possible to grasp objects and accomplish many different tasks.
The hand is linked to the forearm via the carpal bones, which form the area of the wrist.
The wrist and hand share many muscles.
The skeleton of the hand consists of three bony areas.
The carpal bones are located at the top of the hand and consist of eight small bones arranged in two rows of four.
The first row of carpals connects with the bones of the forearm.
The second row of carpals connects with the metacarpal region.
The metacarpal region consists of five long bones called metacarpals.
Each metacarpal connects with phalanges.
The thumb has two phalanges, and the other fingers have three.
The phalanges make up the skeleton of the fingers.
The metacarpals and phalanges flare out like a fan.
Flexion of the wrist moves the palm closer to the anterior surface of the forearm.
In flexion of the wrist, the fingers tend to stretch due to tightening of the flexor tendons.
Extension of the wrist moves the posterior surfaces of the hand and forearm closer together.
In extension of the wrist, the fingers tend to flex due to tightening of the extensor tendons.
You can feel the tightening of the extensor tendons on the back of the hand during wrist extension.
In abduction (or radial deviation), the angle formed by the lateral borders of the hand and forearm decreases, i.e., the thumb moves closer to the radius.
In adduction (or ulnar deviation), the angle formed by the medial borders of the hand and forearm decreases, i.e., the little finger moves closer to the ulna.
The range of motion for adduction is greater than that of abduction.
The wrist and hand usually move in an oblique direction.
Because of the muscles involved, adduction tends to be combined with flexion, whereas abduction tends to occur together with extension.
The wrist, which is only 3cm in height and 5cm in width, consists of two rows, each containing four bones.
The pisiform is a small round bone which sits on the anterior surface of the triquetrum.
The pisiform does not articulate with the forearm nor with the hamate, but does serve for attachment of some ligaments.
The scaphoid articulates superiorly with the radius and inferiorly with the trapezium and trapezoid.
The lunate articulates superiorly with the radius and articular disc and inferiorly with the capitate.
The triquetrum articulates superiorly with the articular disc. Inferiorly, it contacts the hamate and capitate.
The trapezium has a sharp anterior crest. It joins metacarpal I.
The capitate is the largest carpal and has an anterior tubercle. It articulates primarily with metacarpal III and has two facets on the inferior corners which contact metacarpals II and IV.
The hamate has a prominent anterior projection called the 'hook'. The inferior surface of the hamate has two facets oriented in different directions which articulate with metacarpals IV and V.
At the top, the 'radiocarpal' row articulates with the forearm.
At the bottom, the 'metacarpal' row articulates with the metacarpals.
The trapezoid is the most symmetrical of the carpal bones, being shaped like a pyramid with the top cut off. It articulates with metacarpal II.
There are eight carpal bones.
Together, the carpals form an anteriorly-concave arch.
The sides of the arch consist laterally of the scaphoid and trapezium, and medially of the triquetrum, pisiform, and hamate.
Because of the annular (or transverse carpal) ligament which runs anterior to the carpus, this concave space is transformed into a tunnel, the carpal tunnel.
The small intrinsic muscles of the hand and palmaris longus attach above this ligament.
The tendons of the long muscles of the hand pass below it.

Superiorly, the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum present a large convex surface which articulates with the smaller concave surface presented by the distal radius and articular disc to form an ellipsoid joint.
The posterior surface is convex and the bones are bound together here, like on the anterior surface, by many ligaments.
The wrist consists of small bones which articulate with each other laterally.
All the surfaces are covered by cartilage.
There are many small ligaments binding the carpals to one another and to the metacarpals.
The wrist is an articular region consisting of many bones arranged in two rows.
At the top, the radius and the articular disc form the articular radiocarpal surface and articulate with the proximal row of carpal bones (except the pisiform).
This articulation is called the radiocarpal joint.
Below, the proximal row articulates with the second row of carpal bones. This is called the midcarpal joint.
The surface of the radiocarpal articulation has a concave oval shape, whose posterior edge is slightly lower than the anterior edge.
Laterally, it consists of the inferior surface of the radius, and medially of the inferior surface of the articular disc, covered by cartilage.
The articular disc maintains the wrist structure during pronation and supination.
If the wrist were to directly articulate with the two bones of the forearm, it would fold on itself during pronation.
Through the articular disc, the wrist forms a quasi-continuous surface with the radius, whether the forearm is pronated or supinated.
During both movements, it 'wipes' the ulnar head like a windshield wiper.
The midcarpal joint consists of the inferior surfaces of the scaphoid, lunate, and triquetrum proximally, and the superior surfaces of the trapezium, trapezoid, capitate, and hamate distally.
The superior surfaces of the scaphoid and lunate bones, considered together, are the carpal condyle, which articulates with the radius.
These surfaces are covered with cartilage.

The space between the two rows has the shape of the letter S, where we can distinguish two parts: an internal part, which consists of a concave and a convex surface, and an external part, which consists of two flat surfaces superiorly and inferiorly.
The radiocarpal joint is surrounded by a capsule, which is attached to the joint circumference.
It is very loose from front to back, but taut laterally.
It is lined by a synovial membrane.
There is an articular capsule at each midcarpal joint.
The capsules are more or less joined to each other and the synovial membrane is continuous.

The radiocarpal joints have many small ligaments, which can be subdivided into three groups: anterior ligaments run from the anterior surface of the distal radius to the carpal bones, posterior ligaments run from the posterior surface of the distal radius and the articular disc to the carpal bones, and lateral ligaments run from the styloid processes of the radius and ulna to the carpal bones.
The ligaments at the midcarpal joints connect neighboring bones.
They are reinforced by bundles of ligaments from the radiocarpal joint.
Wrist flexion is primarily a function of the radiocarpal joint.
Extension involves the midcarpal joint to a greater extent since the posterior border of the distal radius limits extension at the radiocarpal joint.
In abduction, the scaphoid moves closer to the radius. This movement is limited by the radial styloid.
Medially, the proximal and distal rows of carpals move apart.
The proximal carpal row goes into flexion-pronation while the distal row goes into extension-supination.
In adduction, the triquetrum moves closer to the ulna, while the scaphoid moves away from the radius.
Movement is less restricted here because the ulnar styloid is not as prominent as the radial styloid.
The lateral carpals move apart during adduction.
The movements of the wrist involve two rows of joints.
There are five bony structures which consist of one metacarpal and several phalanges each.
The thumb has two phalanges, and the other fingers have three each.
Each of these bones consists of three parts.
The bony structures studied here are those of fingers 2 through 5.
The structure of the thumb is studied on page 183.
The base of each metacarpal is roughly quadrangular, with facets for articulation with a carpal and the adjacent metacarpals.
The shaft of each metacarpal is roughly triangular, with three surfaces and three sides.
The head of each metacarpal has one cartilaginous articulating surface, which is rounded from front to back and laterally.
On each side of the metacarpal head, there is a small tubercle.
The proximal phalanx of each finger has a concavely rounded base for articulation with the metacarpal and a pulley-shaped head.
The base of the middle phalanx is concave but with a median crest to match the shape of the head of the proximal phalanx.
The head of the middle phalanx has the same surface as the proximal phalanx.
The base of the distal phalanx is identical to the base of the second phalanx.
The palmar head of the distal phalanx has a protuberance for the finger tip area.
These are the joints between the distal row of carpals and the metacarpals.
The articular surfaces of the carpometacarpal joints are straight.
They allow slight sliding/gliding and flexion/extension movements.
The range of these movements increases progressively from metacarpal II through V.
As a result of the anterior curvature of the carpals, the plane of carpometacarpal joints IV and V is oblique to that of joints II and III.
Thus, metacarpals IV and V can flex, which moves them toward the thumb.
It is the carpometacarpal movements as a whole which bring about the anterior depression of the hand.
This depression is made complete by the opposition of the thumb.
These are essentially hinge joints, allowing flexion/extension, abduction/adduction, and rotation.
The range of passive extension is greater than that of active extension.
The joint capsule is slack at the front and back, taut at the sides, and reinforced on the palmar surface by the palmar ligament, a dense band of fibrocartilaginous tissue.
This ligament attaches to the edge of the phalanx and forms a hinge there.
When the joint is extended, the ligament stretches over the surface of the phalangeal base.
When the joint is flexed, it folds due to its hinge-like structure and the folds of the capsule.
The capsule is reinforced by collateral ligaments which run from the tubercle of the metacarpal head to the lateral sides of the base of the phalanx.
Since they originate from the dorsal side of the metacarpal head, which is somewhat narrower than the palmar side, these ligaments are slack in extension and taut in flexion.
Consequently, movements of abduction/adduction and rotation are impossible when the joint is in full flexion.
When the metacarpophalangeal joints are in extension or slight flexion, passive abduction/adduction and rotation allow the hand to adapt itself to grasp a variety of shapes.
When these joints are in a more flexed position, they become less flexible but also more stable, which is helpful for feats requiring strength or force.
The collateral ligaments expand like a fan toward the palmar ligament.
The articular surfaces of interphalangeal joints can be compared to a convex double-track, which articulates with a concave double-track.
Interphalangeal joints allow for anterior and posterior movements in the sagittal plane.
The capsule and ligaments of interphalangeal joints are arranged similarly to metacarpophalangeal joints.
Flexion is possible between the proximal and middle phalanx of a finger.
Flexion is possible between the middle and distal phalanx of a finger.
Extension is possible between the middle and distal phalanx of a finger, but usually within a limited range.
Extension is usually limited to 180°.
Muscles that directly move the wrist are shown in regular type.
Muscles that move the fingers and indirectly the wrist are shown in italic.
The palmaris longus muscle originates on the humerus.
The flexor carpi radialis muscle originates on the humerus.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle originates on the humerus.
The flexor digitorum superficialis muscle originates on the humerus.
The extensor carpi radialis longus muscle originates on the humerus.
The extensor carpi radialis brevis muscle originates on the humerus.
The extensor digitorum muscle originates on the humerus.
The extensor digiti minimi muscle originates on the humerus.
The extensor carpi ulnaris muscle originates on the humerus.
The flexor digitorum superficialis muscle originates on the radius.
The flexor policis longus muscle originates on the radius.
The abductor policis longus muscle originates on the radius.
The flexor digitorum profundus muscle originates on the ulna.
The flexor digitorum superficialis muscle originates on the ulna.
The flexor policis longus muscle originates on the ulna.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle originates on the ulna.
The abductor pollicis longus muscle originates on the ulna.
The extensor pollicis longus muscle originates on the ulna.
The extensor pollicis brevis muscle originates on the ulna.
The extensor indicis muscle originates on the ulna.
The extensor carpi ulnaris muscle originates on the ulna.
The palmaris longus muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The flexor carpi radialis muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The extensor carpi radialis longus muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The extensor carpi radialis brevis muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The extensor carpi ulnaris muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The abductor pollicis longus muscle attaches to the carpal and metacarpal joints.
The flexor digitorum profundus muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The flexor digitorum superficialis muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The flexor policis longus muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The extensor pollicis longus muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The extensor pollicis brevis muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The extensor digitorum muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The extensor indicis muscle attaches to the phalanges.
The extensor digiti minimi muscle attaches to the phalanges.
There are three flexor muscles located at the anterior surface of the forearm.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle originates at the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle of the humerus.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle descends along the medial ulna and styloid process.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle inserts on the pisiform and hamate.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle flexes and adducts the wrist.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle plays a very minor role in assisting flexion of the elbow.
The flexor carpi ulnaris muscle is innervated by the ulnar nerve (C7-C8).
The palmaris longus arises from the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle of the humerus and inserts on the flexor retinaculum and palmar aponeurosis.
The palmaris longus flexes the wrist and assists weakly in elbow flexion.
The palmaris longus is innervated by the median nerve (C7-C8).
The flexor carpi radialis arises from the common flexor origin at the medial epicondyle of the humerus and then runs down the forearm.
The tendon of the flexor carpi radialis runs through the carpal tunnel and inserts on the bases of metacarpals II and III.
The flexor carpi radialis flexes and abducts the wrist, acting on both the radiocarpal and midcarpal joints.
The flexor carpi radialis assists weakly in elbow flexion and pronation.
The flexor carpi radialis is innervated by the median nerve (C6-C7).
The extensor carpi radialis longus originates from the lateral epicondyle (common extensor origin) and supracondylar ridge of the humerus.
The tendon of the extensor carpi radialis longus passes under the extensor retinaculum and inserts on the posterior base of metacarpal II.
The extensor carpi radialis brevis arises from the common extensor origin and inserts on the posterior base of metacarpal III.
The extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis extend the wrist and participate in elbow flexion.
The extensor carpi radialis longus and brevis are innervated by the radial nerve (C7-C8 and C6-C7 respectively).
The extensor carpi ulnaris originates from the common extensor origin and the posterior border of the ulna.
The extensor carpi ulnaris passes under the extensor retinaculum and inserts on the posterior base of metacarpal V.
The extensor carpi ulnaris extends and adducts the wrist and participates weakly in elbow extension.
The extensor carpi ulnaris is innervated by the radial nerve (C7-C8).
Extrinsic flexors of the fingers are two muscles whose mass is arranged on top of each other on the anterior surface of the forearm and whose tendons end on the phalanges.
Flexor digitorum profundus has a broad origin on the anterior and medial ulna and the medial half of the interosseous membrane which connects the ulna and radius.
Flexor digitorum profundus splits into four tendons which pass through the carpal tunnel and insert on the distal phalanges of fingers II through V.
At the level of the metacarpals, the tendons of flexor digitorum profundus attach to the lumbrical muscles.
Flexor digitorum profundus actions are flexing the third phalanx toward the second and participating in flexion of the other two phalanges.
Flexor digitorum profundus is innervated by the median and ulnar nerves (C7-T1).
Flexor digitorum superficialis has two heads: one from the common flexor origin and the coronoid process of the ulna, and the other from the anterior surface of the radius.

Flexor digitorum superficialis splits into four tendons which pass through the carpal tunnel (superficial to the tendons of flexor digitorum profundus), split into "Y" shapes to accommodate passage of other flexor tendons, and inserts bilaterally on the middle phalanges of fingers II through V.
Flexor digitorum superficialis actions are flexing the second phalanx toward the first and, due to the fibrous sheath, flexing the first phalanx on the metacarpals.
Flexor digitorum superficialis assists in flexion of the wrist and plays a weak role in elbow flexion.
Flexor digitorum superficialis is innervated by the median nerve (C7-T1).
Extensor digitorum arises from the common extensor origin, passes down the back of the forearm, and splits into four tendons.

Each tendon of extensor digitorum splits into three bands, of which the central band inserts on the posterior base of the proximal and middle phalanges while the two lateral bands reunite at the base of the distal phalanx.
Extensor digitorum, together with the lumbricals and interosseous muscles, assists in extension of the interphalangeal joints.
Extrinsic extensors of the fingers are three muscles located on the posterior side of the forearm. Their tendons insert on the posterior side of the hand.
Extrinsic extensors of the fingers are innervated by the radial nerve (C6-C8).
The extensor indicis arises from the posterior ulna and interosseous membrane, below the origin of extensor pollicis longus.
The tendon of the extensor indicis joins that of extensor digitorum, leading to the index finger.
The action of the extensor indicis reinforces the action of extensor digitorum on the level of the index finger.
The extensor indicis is innervated by the radial nerve (C6-C8).
The extensor digiti minimi originates from the common extensor origin.
The tendon of the extensor digiti minimi joins that of extensor digitorum, leading to the little finger.
The action of the extensor digiti minimi reinforces the action of extensor digitorum on the level of the little finger.
The extensor digiti minimi is innervated by the radial nerve (C6-C8).
The interossei are small muscles originating from the metacarpals.
The interossei occupy the spaces between the metacarpals.
There are four dorsal interossei originating close to the back of the hand.
The major tendon of the dorsal interossei consists of two parts.
One part of the dorsal interossei tendon inserts on the base of the proximal phalanx (the lateral tubercle).
Another part of the dorsal interossei tendon consists of three fibers.
The first fiber of the second part of the dorsal interossei tendon skirts the phalanx and joins the identical fibers on the adjacent interosseous.
The second and third fibers of the second part of the dorsal interossei tendon insert on the edges of the extensor digitorum tendon at the level of the proximal and middle phalanges.
There are three palmar interossei originating closer to the palm of the hand.
Acting together bilaterally, the first part of the tendon and the first fiber of the second part of the dorsal interossei tendon flex the proximal phalanx.
The two fibers that insert on the extensor tendon pull on the proximal phalanx and extend the middle phalanx toward the proximal, and the distal phalanx toward the middle.
The four lumbricals originate from the tendons of flexor digitorum profundus.
The lumbricals insert on the tendons of extensor digitorum.
Collectively, the lumbricals flex the metacarpophalangeal joints and extend the interphalangeal joints.
The lumbricals are innervated by the ulnar nerve (C8-T1).
The dorsal interossei abduct the fingers.
The palmar interossei adduct the fingers.
The dorsal interossei and palmar interossei are innervated by the ulnar nerve (C8-T1).
The bodies of the hypothenar muscles provide the bulk of the hypothenar eminence on the medial side of the palm.
Opponens digiti minimi originates from the flexor retinaculum and hamate hook, and inserts on the medial surface of metacarpal V.
Opponens digiti minimi helps move the little finger toward the thumb (for grasping) and create the curvature of the palm.
Opponens digiti minimi is innervated by the ulnar nerve (C8-T1).
Opponens digiti minimi flexes the little finger.
Abductor digiti minimi arises from the pisiform and flexor retinaculum, and inserts in the same place as flexor digiti minimi.
Abductor digiti minimi abducts the little finger and flexes its proximal phalanx.
Abductor digiti minimi is innervated by the ulnar nerve (C8-T1).
Flexor digiti minimi has the same origin as opponens and inserts on the base of the proximal phalanx of finger V.
The inferior surface of the trapezium is concave across and convex from front to back.
The superior surface of metacarpal I, which articulates with it, is concave from front to back and convex across.
The trapezium and metacarpal I form a saddle joint.
The saddle joint allows the thumb to move through the three planes of movement described on pages 8-10.
The thumb can be moved to oppose the other fingers, allowing grasping and manipulation of objects.
The thumb has the same mobility in its metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints as the other fingers.
The first metacarpal is positioned at a 20° angle to the second metacarpal and placed at a 40° angle anteriorly.
In a hand at rest, the thumb faces the other fingers at a right angle.
In extension, the metacarpal moves posterolaterally, while in flexion it moves anteromedially, closer to the palm.
In abduction, the thumb moves anterolaterally.
In adduction, the thumb moves posteromedially.
The capsule of carpometacarpal joint I is slack, allowing some axial rotation in addition to the movements described above.
Metacarpophalangeal joint I differs from II through V in a few respects.
Metacarpophalangeal joint I is more massive than joints II through V.
The capsule of Metacarpophalangeal joint I is not as taut and allows some axial rotation.
Two small sesamoid bones are embedded in the palmar fascia of Metacarpophalangeal joint I and serve for tendon attachment.
The interphalangeal joint of the thumb is similar to those of fingers II through V, except for being more massive.
Flexor pollicis longus originates from the anterior radius.
The tendon of Flexor pollicis longus passes through the carpal tunnel and inserts on the base of the distal phalanx of the thumb.
Flexor pollicis longus flexes the interphalangeal joint I, metacarpophalangeal joint I, and carpometacarpal joint I.
Flexor pollicis longus assists in flexion of the wrist and abduction (radial deviation).
Flexor pollicis longus is innervated by the anterior interosseous nerve (C7-C8).
Abductor pollicis longus arises from the posterior surfaces of the ulna, radius, and interosseous ligament, inferior to supinator.
The tendon of Abductor pollicis longus passes under the extensor retinaculum and inserts on the lateral base of metacarpal I.
Abductor pollicis longus performs anteromedial movement of the thumb.
Abductor pollicis longus also assists in flexion of the wrist and abduction (radial deviation).
Abductor pollicis longus is innervated by the radial nerve (C7-C8).
Extensor pollicis brevis originates inferior to abductor pollicis longus.
The tendon of Extensor pollicis brevis inserts on the base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb.
Extensor pollicis brevis extends the metacarpophalangeal and carpometacarpal joints of the thumb.
Extensor pollicis brevis assists in abduction of the thumb.
Extensor pollicis brevis is innervated by the radial nerve (C7-T1).
Extensor pollicis longus arises on the posterior ulna and interosseous membrane, inferior to extensor pollicis brevis.
Extensor pollicis longus inserts on the base of the distal phalanx of the thumb.
Extensor pollicis longus extends the metacarpophalangeal and carpometacarpal joints of the thumb.
Extensor pollicis longus also extends the interphalangeal joint of the thumb.
Extensor pollicis longus is innervated by the radial nerve (C7-C8).
When the thumb is fully extended, a depression known as the 'anatomical snuffbox' can be seen at the posterior base of the thumb.
The anatomical snuffbox is bordered laterally by the tendons of abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis.
The anatomical snuffbox is bordered medially by the tendon of extensor pollicis longus.
Adductor pollicis has two fibers: Adductor pollicis obliquus arises from the trapezoid and capitate bones.
Adductor pollicis transversus arises from the 2nd and 3rd metacarpals and the corresponding metacarpophalangeal joint.
The two fibers of the adductor pollicis insert on the medial base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb, and the medial sesamoid bone located at metacarpophalangeal joint I.
Adductor pollicis moves the 2nd metacarpal toward the 1st; it also flexes the metacarpophalangeal joint.
The innervation of the adductor pollicis is the ulnar nerve (C8-T1).
Flexor pollicis brevis consists of two layers: a deep layer which arises from the trapezoid and capitate, and a superficial layer which arises from the trapezium and the flexor retinaculum.

The two layers of flexor pollicis brevis merge into one tendon, which inserts on the lateral base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb, and the lateral sesamoid bone located at metacarpophalangeal joint I.
Flexor pollicis brevis moves the metacarpals anteromedially and in medial rotation, and flexes the proximal phalanx of the thumb.
The innervation of flexor pollicis brevis is the median and ulnar nerves (C8-T1).
Opponens pollicis arises from the crest of the trapezium and the flexor retinaculum on the anterior medial surface of the first metacarpal.
Opponens pollicis inserts on the lateral shaft of metacarpal I.
Opponens pollicis causes anteromedial movement of the first metacarpal, causing a strong medial rotation; it is important in grasping movements.
The innervation of opponens pollicis is the median nerve (C6-C7).
Abductor pollicis brevis arises from the flexor retinaculum, scaphoid, and trapezium, and inserts on the lateral base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb next to flexor pollicis brevis.
Abductor pollicis brevis pulls the metacarpal anteriorly and flexes the metacarpophalangeal joint.
The innervation of abductor pollicis brevis is the median nerve (C8-T1).
The hip is the proximal joint of the lower limbs and connects the femur to the pelvis.
The hip is surrounded by thick muscles, making it difficult to palpate or localize.
The stability and powerful musculature of the hip are essential for standing and walking.
Many physical disciplines require good range of motion (ROM) at the hip.
Restrictions in ROM at the hip are common and typically affect nearby structures such as the lower back, knee, and foot joints.
The knee is an intermediate joint on the lower limb and is primarily capable of flexion and extension.
The mobility of the knee allows it to vary the distance between the foot and the trunk to a large degree.
The stability of the knee is not due to bone structure, which is rather weak, but rather to the arrangement of ligaments and muscles.
The knee receives considerable stress, both from above (body weight) and below (impact of the foot on the ground, footwear).
This chapter looks at the hip and knee together because these two joints have many muscles in common.
The shape of the hip joint's articulations allows it to be moved in many directions.
For ease of study, the movements of the hip are described with respect to the planes they intersect.
Hip flexion is the movement in which the angle between the anterior surfaces of the thigh and the trunk decreases.
ROM for hip flexion is greater when the knee is also flexed.
ROM for passive flexion is greater than for active flexion since the flexing muscles are relaxed and can be compressed.
When the knee is extended, hip flexion is restricted by the limits of elasticity of the hamstring muscles.
Hip flexion is often associated with extension of the pelvis.
In hip extension, the angle between the posterior surfaces of the thigh and the trunk decreases.
ROM for extension is limited compared to that for flexion.
In the 'grande arabesque,' extension is combined with lateral rotation of the hip.
Flexion and rotation of the pelvis on the opposite side can give an impression of extension.
In adduction of the hip, the thigh moves toward or past the median plane.
Adduction of the hip can be combined with slight flexion or extension.
ROM for hip extension is greater when the knee is extended, but is reduced when the knee is flexed, because of the limits of elasticity of the rectus femoris muscle.
In abduction of the hip, the thigh moves away from the median plane, and the angle between the lateral surfaces of the thigh and the trunk decreases.
When the femur is in neutral or medial rotation, abduction is limited to about 40° because of contact between the superior femoral neck and the upper edge of the acetabulum.
With the femur in lateral rotation, the inferior aspect of the neck faces the edge of the socket, and ROM for abduction is greater.
In medial rotation of the hip, the femur rotates on its own long axis, and the toes of the foot (or an imaginary spot on the front of the thigh) move closer to the median plane.
Good ROM for medial hip rotation is needed to assume the position shown at left without forcing lateral rotation at the knee joint.
In lateral rotation of the hip, the toes (or a spot on the front of the thigh) move away from the median plane.
Good ROM for lateral rotation is needed for the 'en dehors' position of ballet or for assuming the 'lotus position' without stressing the knee and ankle joints.
When the hip is flexed, ROM for lateral rotation is greater because the ilio-femoral ligament is slack.
Most often, the hip movements described here combine several directions in one movement, e.g., abduction + lateral rotation, or flexion + abduction.
The anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) can move forward (flexion of the pelvis), which tends to increase lordosis of the lumbar spine.
The ASIS can move backward (extension of the pelvis), which decreases lumbar lordosis.
The ASIS can move inferolaterally (lateral flexion).
The ASIS can undergo limited medial rotation of the pelvis.
When done in an upright position, medial rotation of the pelvis and lateral rotation of the pelvis are associated with sidebending of the lumbar spine.
The illustrations depict the movement of the pelvis on the supported hip and not on the unsupported hip.
The greater trochanter has five surfaces: anterior, lateral, posterior, medial, and superior.
Many deep thigh muscles attach to the greater trochanter.
The femur is the longest and heaviest bone in the human body.
The superior end of the femur is the head, which has a smooth, spherical surface.
The neck is just distal to the head.
The greater trochanter and lesser trochanter are roughened projections serving for muscle attachment.
The shaft of the femur is roughly triangular in cross section.
The distal end (epiphysis) of the femur is greatly expanded and forms part of the knee joint.
On the posterior shaft of the femur is a prominent double ridge, the linea aspera, to which nine muscles attach.
The linea aspera is bifurcated superiorly and inferiorly.
Viewed from the side, the shaft of the femur has a slight concave bent toward the back.
On the lateral surface of the hip bones is the acetabulum (Latin for 'small bowl'), a deep socket formed by the junction of the ilium, pubis, and ischium.
The acetabulum should not be confused with the obturator foramen.
The articular surface of the hip only constitutes part of the acetabulum.
The articular surface of the hip is shaped like a croissant.
This is the lunate surface of the acetabulum.
The deeper portion of the acetabulum does not articulate with the femur.
The ligamentum teres is located in the deeper portion of the acetabulum.
Anteroinferiorly, the surface of the acetabulum is interrupted (at the ends of the croissant).
The acetabulum is directed laterally, anteriorly, and inferiorly because of the structure of the pelvis.
The upper part of the acetabulum socket, which fits against the head of the femur, is slightly oblique rather than horizontal.
The angle of the acetabulum varies with the individual and with age.
The more the acetabulum angle deviates from horizontal, the less stable is the hip joint.
The femoral head, the articulating surface of the femur, represents about two-thirds of a sphere.
The femoral head is covered with thick cartilage except at the fovea.
The ligamentum teres connects the femoral head to the acetabulum and is attached at the fovea.
Viewed from the front, the femoral neck is oblique and points superomedially.
Viewed from the top, the femoral neck is oblique and points anteromedially.
The precise angle of the femoral head and the length of the femoral neck vary with the individual and with age.
A fibrocartilaginous ring called the labrum is attached around the rim of the acetabulum.
A transverse acetabular ligament reinforces the labrum and bridges the inferior opening of the notch.
The labrum helps hold the femoral head in place and increases the effective depth of the socket.
The head of the femur sits on top of the femoral neck.
The surfaces of the hip, with the additional fibrocartilaginous ring, form an 'encased' articular structure.
In anatomical position, the anterior femoral head is partly exposed.
The head fits better into the socket when the femur is flexed to a 90° angle relative to the trunk.
Maximal contact between the articular surfaces is attained by a combination of flexion, abduction, and lateral rotation.
We often assume this position spontaneously when at rest.
The average angle between the femoral neck and shaft is 135°.
A smaller angle between the femoral neck and shaft, called coxa vara, reduces the range of abduction.
A greater angle between the femoral neck and shaft than 135° (coxa valga) increases the range of abduction.
Seen from above, the neck is oriented anteriorly at an angle of 10 to 30°.
When the anteversion angle is small, the head fits well into the socket in anatomical position and maintains good articular contact even in lateral rotation.
When the anteversion angle is large, the anterior part of the head is more exposed in anatomical position, and the posterior part loses contact with the socket in lateral rotation.
Lateral rotation is more restricted in individuals with a large anteversion angle due to contact between the neck and the lateral edge of the acetabulum.
A more concave and longer femoral neck facilitates abduction and lateral rotation.
A shorter, less concave femoral neck restricts abduction and lateral rotation due to contact with the edge of the acetabulum.
There are intrinsic limitations to range of motion (ROM) at the hip joint due to the shape of the articulating bones, which may vary between individuals.
The capsule of the hip joint attaches firmly around the rim of the acetabulum and at the base of the femoral neck.
The hip joint capsule is thick, tough, and reinforced by ligaments.
Two anterior ligaments consist of three fasciae forming an N-shape: the superior and middle fasciae of the iliofemoral ligament and the fascia of the pubofemoral ligament.
The ischiofemoral ligament is arranged posteriorly in a spiral shape and is weaker than the other ligaments.
Deep circular fibers reinforce the middle of the capsule, giving it an hourglass shape.
The iliofemoral (both branches) and pubofemoral ligaments become slack in flexion and taut in extension.
In abduction, the upper iliofemoral ligament is slack while the pubofemoral ligament is taut.
In adduction, the opposite occurs compared to abduction.
All ligaments become taut in lateral rotation and slack in medial rotation.
Flexion and medial rotation loosen the ligaments, while extension and lateral rotation make them taut.
The knee is primarily a hinge joint.
Flexion decreases the angle formed by the posterior thigh and leg.
ROM for active flexion is limited by contact between the bodies of the contracting muscles.
ROM for passive flexion is greater (i.e., the heel can touch the buttock) since the flexor muscles are relaxed and more easily compressed.
The extensor muscles are passively stretched.
ROM for flexion is greater when the hip joint is flexed and smaller when the hip is extended.
Extension is an increase in the angle between the posterior thigh and leg, i.e., a return from flexion back to anatomical position.
Hyperextension, or 'genu recurvatum' (an increase of this angle significantly beyond 180°), is uncommon.
ROM of knee extension is greater when the hip is extended and limited when the hip is flexed.
To describe rotation, we will assume that the femur is fixed.
In lateral rotation, the tuberosity moves laterally.
In medial rotation, the tibial tuberosity moves medially.
Rotation can occur to an appreciable extent only when the knee is flexed, and the ligaments are relaxed.
If the knee is extended and you see the tuberosity moving medially or laterally, this is rotation not at the knee but at the hip.
These rotations occur automatically during knee flexion and extension, although ROM is small then and involves both bones (not just the mobile tibia below the femur, as shown above).
These rotations are primarily due to the shape of the articular surfaces.
The knee joint consists of three bones.
The femur articulates with the patella, which is called the femoropatellar joint.
The femur articulates with the tibia, which is called the femorotibial joint.
The patella does not articulate with the tibia.
The shaft of the tibia is triangular in cross section.
The shaft of the femur is triangular in cross section (see p. 200).

At the bottom of the femur, the posterior edge of the femur's distal end bifurcates such that its shape in cross section changes to a square, which expands; thus, the base of the femur looks like the trunk of a pyramid.
At the top of the tibia, the anterior edge of the tibia's proximal end also bifurcates and changes to an expanding square shape. Its top looks like an upside-down pyramid.
The two bones are both expanded where they come together and form a massive structure, like the ends of two columns.
The fibers of the alveolar (spongy) tissue inside are oriented diagonally and vertically, as well as horizontally, which increases their strength.
Above and posterior to the two condyles is a bony tuberosity.
The condyles are less curved anteriorly (good for weight-bearing function).
The base of the pyramid is a rounded articular surface, shaped roughly like a pulley.
The anterior portion is called the intercondylar fossa of the femur, which articulates with the patella.
Posteroinferiorly, the single-track pulley becomes double-track, and the shape of the surfaces is like the legs of a rocking chair.
These are the medial and lateral condyles of the femur, which articulate with the condyles of the tibia.
The medial condyle is more curved than the lateral condyle, which helps explain the automatic rotations of the knee during flexion/extension (see p. 223).
Prolonged standing with the knee in slightly flexed position puts stress on the small articular surface of the condyles, and can damage the cartilage.
The superior surface (base of the pyramid) of the tibia is called the tibial plateau.
The lateral and medial condyles of the tibia, protected by cartilage, are concave for articulation with the convex condyles of the femur.
The lateral surface of the tibial plateau has a tubercle, called Gerdy's tubercle, where the fascia lata inserts.
The anterior surface of the tibial plateau has a prominence, the anterior tibial tuberosity, where the quadriceps muscle inserts. You can feel this area when you kneel.
At the center of the tibial plateau, the edges of the condyles are raised and form the intercondylar eminence.
Anterior and posterior to the intercondylar eminence are two hollow surfaces, which do not articulate with the femur.
The sartorius, semitendinosus, and rectus femoris muscles attach to the upper medial shaft of the tibia. This is also where the tibial collateral ligament attaches.
The tibial condyles are concave transversely, but from front to back the lateral condyle is slightly convex while the medial one is concave.
The femorotibial articulation looks like a double-wheel structure fitting into a set of two tracks.
During knee flexion, if the femoral condyles simply rolled backward, the femur would slip off the tibia.
There are two mechanisms associated with the movements of flexion and extension of the knee: rolling and gliding.
If the knee were to glide in one spot like a ball bearing, a single spot on the tibia would receive all the friction, and the cartilage there would be damaged.
The movement of the knee in the sagittal plane can therefore be described as a rolling-gliding movement.
The opposite occurs in extension of the knee: first gliding, then rolling.
During knee extension, the lateral condyle rolls more than the medial condyle, which leads to automatic rotation of the knee.
In flexion, the femoral condyle first rolls (15-20°) on the tibial condyle, then glides.
For the lower limb, in anatomical position, we can consider three different axes.
The first ('mechanical axis') passes through the middle of the femoral head above and the middle of the ankle joint below.
In anatomical position, this axis is at an angle of about 3° from a vertical (sagittal) plane.
The second and third axes are those passing through the shafts of the femur and tibia, which form an angle that is usually between 170° and 175°.
The lateral angle formed by these two axes varies from person to person.
If you stand on one foot, this axis moves farther from the sagittal plane.
It can be less than 180° (genu valgum or 'knock knees'), or greater than 180° (genu varum or 'bow-legs').
The menisci (singular: meniscus) are two croissant-shaped intra-articular discs made of fibrocartilage.
Their shallow central tips are attached to the intercondylar eminence of the tibia, and their thicker margins are attached to the peripheral edges of the medial and lateral tibial condyles.
They also have attachments to nearby structures such as the meniscopatellar ligaments, medial collateral ligament, and tendons of the popliteus and semimembranosus muscles.
The menisci are slightly mobile and aid in spreading the synovial fluid during movements of the knee.
The menisci increase the distribution of synovial fluid as they move around.
The menisci increase the weight-bearing surface, resulting in a better distribution of pressure as they move around.
The menisci increase the concave shape of the tibial condyles, providing better stability.

In knee extension, the menisci move forward because they are pushed in that direction by the femoral condyles and pulled by the meniscopatellar ligaments, which are in turn pulled upward by movement of the patella.

In knee flexion, the menisci move backward because they are pushed in that direction by the condyles and pulled by the tendons of the semimembranosus and popliteus muscles, and the medial collateral ligament.
In knee rotation, the ipsilateral meniscus moves forward because of pressure from the condyle and is held back by the meniscopatellar ligament.
These movements of the menisci are all necessary for normal function of the knee joint.
In some cases, particularly rapid extension movements, as in soccer, the menisci may not move fast enough and become crushed or torn.
