The knee joint is held by a thick capsule that attaches just outside the articular surfaces of the three bones involved.
The patella is contained in the anterior capsule.
The patella, femur, tibia, and capsule enclose a single synovial cavity within which synovial fluid circulates.
The knee capsule is very slack anteriorly, which allows good range of motion for flexion.
In knee extension, the capsule forms deep folds at the front and sides.
In cases of prolonged immobilization of the joint, these folds can develop adhesions which subsequently limit flexion.
In terms of bone morphology, the knee is not a very well-fitted joint, therefore its ligaments are essential to its stability.
The patella is attached to the femoral condyles and the menisci by small ligaments (actually thickenings of the capsule).

The strong patellar ligament, which contains the patella and inserts on the tibial tuberosity, can be viewed as a continuation of the tendon of the quadriceps muscle, whose fibers cross over each other at the knee joint.
Posteriorly, the knee capsule is thicker and forms two strong bands connecting the femoral and tibial condyles.
These bands resist hyperextension of the joint and provide 'passive stability' in the standing position.
The joint is also held in place by two cruciate ('crossed') ligaments located in the intercondylar fossa of the femur.
The anterior cruciate ligament is attached to the anterior intercondylar area of the tibia and runs posterosuperolaterally, attaching to the medial aspect of the lateral femoral condyle.
The posterior cruciate ligament attaches to the posterior intercondylar area of the tibia and runs anterosuperomedially, attaching to the lateral surface of the medial femoral condyle.
The principal role of the cruciate ligaments is to prevent anteroposterior displacements of the two bones.
The anterior cruciate ligament tends to resist anterior displacement of the tibia on the femur, while the posterior cruciate ligament resists posterior displacement.
In both flexion and extension, the cruciate ligaments remain fairly taut, and displacement of the tibia on the femur is minimal.
In lateral rotation, the cruciates slacken somewhat.
Because simple anterior and posterior ligaments would not allow flexion.
In medial rotation, they press against each other and therefore become more taut.
The joint capsule of the hip and knee is reinforced by two collateral ligaments.
The lower attachment of the medial collateral ligament is slightly anterior relative to its upper attachment.
The principal function of the medial collateral ligament is to stabilize the joint and prevent it from opening on the medial side.
If the medial collateral ligament is ruptured, the tibia will be able to move laterally.
The lateral (fibular) collateral ligament runs from the lateral condyle of the femur to the head of the fibula.
The lower attachment of the lateral collateral ligament is slightly posterior relative to its upper attachment.
The principal function of the lateral collateral ligament is to prevent the joint from opening on the lateral side.
If the lateral collateral ligament is ruptured, the tibia will be able to move medially.
The medial (tibial) collateral ligament runs from the medial condyle of the femur to the medial condyle and upper medial shaft of the tibia.
The medial collateral ligament is considerably thicker and stronger than its lateral counterpart.
In the average person, the lateral angle formed by the femur and tibia is slightly less than 180° (genu valgum).
Since the joint 'gapes' more on the medial side, there is a need for stronger stabilization on that side.
The collaterals tend to be taut in extension and slack in flexion.
Therefore, they resist hyperextension.
The collaterals become slack in medial rotation of the tibia due to their orientation and taut in lateral rotation.
Thus, they resist excessive lateral rotation of the tibia.
The knee ligaments act together to stabilize the joint.
In extension, all the ligaments are taut, and the joint can be passively stabilized without any muscular action.
Here, the joint is 'locked' in slight hyperextension by the tautness of the ligaments (particularly the thickened posterior portion of the joint capsule).
In flexion, most of the ligaments are slack, and the joint therefore has some capacity for rotation.
The collaterals and cruciates tend to limit lateral and medial rotation, respectively.
But they are more restrictive in the extended than in the flexed position.
While balancing on one foot with the knee flexed, several muscles are used to stabilize the body.
The quadriceps prevent the knee from flexing more.
Rotator muscles prevent or slow down excessive rotation medially: vastus medialis, sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus laterally: vastus medialis, biceps femoris, tensor fascia latae.
Some automatic rotation of the knee occurs during flexion/extension.
The primary explanation for this rotation involves the shape of the femoral and tibial condyles.
The medial femoral condyle is more curved than the lateral one.
During extension, the shaft of the femur is directed forward.
During flexion, due to the shape of the cone, the landmark becomes directed somewhat laterally.
The tibial condyles are also not totally symmetric; both are concave transversely, but from front to back the lateral condyle is slightly convex while the medial one is concave.
Therefore, the lateral tibial condyle allows more rolling than does the medial one.
During flexion, the lateral femoral condyle rolls backward more than the medial one does, which accentuates the lateral orientation of our landmark, i.e., the lateral rotation of the femur.
The secondary explanation for automatic rotation of the knee is that the medial collateral ligament is stronger than the lateral one.
This reinforces the tendency of the medial femoral condyle to be less mobile than the lateral one.
The patella is a sesamoid bone located anterior to the distal end of the femur.
It develops within the tendon of the quadriceps muscle.
Its anterior surface sits directly beneath the skin and is easily palpable.
The two articular facets on its posterior surface fit against the patellar surface of the femur and are separated by a vertical ridge.
The patella is both attached to the knee and is mobile on top of it.
It is connected to the femoral condyles via the lateral and medial patellar ligaments.
It is connected to the menisci via the meniscopatellar ligaments.
It is connected with the tendon of the quadriceps femoris via the patellar ligament.
The primary function of the patella is to protect the quadriceps tendon.
During movement, this tendon slides in the groove between the femoral condyles, like a rope in a pulley.
The patella experiences strain from being pressed against the groove during flexion due to the pulling action of the quadriceps muscles.
This pressure can be 400 kg or more during squatting, and even more when carrying a heavy object.
The patella also experiences strain from stretching (pulling forces from different directions) and strain from constant usage.
The patella is not stable laterally.
The quadriceps follows the femoral shaft and its force is slightly oblique, but its tendon runs straight down to insert on the tibia.
Thus, contraction of the quadriceps tends to pull the patella laterally.

The lateral instability of the patella is maximal during active extension or slight flexion when the patella is not firmly pressed against the patellar surface of the femur; during full flexion the patella is better 'locked' in place.
This instability is accentuated during lateral rotation of the tibia when the lower as well as upper part of the tendon become obliquely oriented.

The tendency of the patella to move laterally is counteracted by two mechanisms: the projection of the lateral femoral condyle, which is more pronounced than that of the medial condyle, and the contraction of the vastus medialis muscle, which pulls the tendon medially.
The articulation of the patella against the femur is subjected to major strains and stresses, particularly on the lateral side.
This explains the frequency of arthritis here, which can compromise proper gliding of the patella and active extension of the knee.
If the sacrum is fixed, the piriformis laterally rotates, abducts, and flexes the femur.
If the femur is fixed, the piriformis contributes to extension of the pelvis (bilateral contraction) or to medial rotation of the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
The piriformis is innervated by the sacral plexus (L5-S2).
The piriformis originates on the anterior sacrum, passes under the greater sciatic notch, and inserts on the top of the greater trochanter.
If the pelvis is fixed, the quadratus femoris laterally rotates the thigh.
If the femur is fixed, the quadratus femoris contributes to extension of the pelvis (bilateral contraction) or to medial rotation of the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
The quadratus femoris is innervated by the inferior gluteal nerve and the sacral plexus (L5-S2).
The quadratus femoris runs from the lateral ischium, posterior to the obturator foramen, and horizontally to the posterior aspect of the greater trochanter.
The following four muscles insert into the medial surface of the greater trochanter, at the level of a depression called the trochanteric fossa: obturator internus.
The obturator internus arises from the obturator membrane and adjacent portions of the ischium and ilium.
The fibers of the obturator internus pass posteriorly through the lesser sciatic notch, make a sharp bend around the body of the ischium, and insert on the medial aspect of the greater trochanter.
There is a bursa where the obturator internus wraps around the ischium to reduce friction.
If the pelvis is fixed, the obturator internus laterally rotates, flexes, and abducts the thigh.
If the femur is fixed, the obturator internus acts in extension (bilateral contraction) and in medial rotation or medial flexion (unilateral contraction) of the pelvis.
The obturator internus is innervated by the inferior gluteal nerve and the sacral plexus (L5-S2).
The gemellus superior and inferior muscles are located above and below the distal borders of the obturator internus, at the level of the lesser sciatic notch.
The gemellus superior and inferior muscles reinforce the actions of the obturator internus.
The obturator externus muscle arises from the external surface of the obturator membrane, passes posterior to the femoral neck, and inserts on a fossa on the medial surface of the greater trochanter.
If the pelvis is fixed, the obturator externus muscle laterally rotates, flexes, and abducts the femur.
If the femur is fixed, the obturator externus muscle functions in flexion of the pelvis (bilateral contraction).
The obturator externus muscle also medially rotates and flexes the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
The obturator externus muscle is innervated by the obturator nerve (L1-L4).
Obturator internus and the gemelli muscles run from the greater trochanter in a posteroinferior direction, while the obturator externus muscle runs anteroinferiorly.
If the pelvis is fixed, the combined action of the obturators and gemelli muscles pulls the femur down relative to the pelvis.
If the femur is fixed, the obturators and gemelli muscles lift the pelvis relative to the femur.
The obturators and gemelli muscles tend to 'pull apart' the hip joint, on a very small scale.
The obturators and gemelli muscles have a decompressive effect which is beneficial for certain painful conditions (e.g., worn-down cartilage).
The psoas major muscle arises from the bodies of T12 through L5, and from arches of fascia which connect the bony parts of the vertebral bodies but do not attach to the intervertebral disks.
The psoas major muscle runs anterior to the pelvis, posterior to the inguinal ligament, and inserts on the lesser trochanter.
There is a bursa to reduce friction where the psoas major muscle bends at the anterior pelvis.
When the vertebrae are fixed, the psoas major muscle flexes the hip, and works as a weak adductor and lateral rotator.
The psoas major muscle is innervated by the lumbar plexus, femoral nerve (L1-L3).
The iliacus muscle arises from the entire internal iliac fossa.
Inferiorly, the fibers of the iliacus muscle merge with those of the psoas and insert on the lesser trochanter via the same tendon.
When the pelvis is fixed, the action of the iliacus muscle is identical to that of the psoas muscle in flexing the hip.
When the femur is fixed, the iliacus muscle acts in flexion of the pelvis (bilateral contraction).
The innervation of the iliacus muscle is the lumbar plexus, femoral nerve (L2-L4).
The superior attachments of the iliacus and psoas muscles are quite different.
When the femur is fixed, the iliacus muscle acts on the pelvis, whereas the psoas muscle acts on the lumbar spine.
Because the iliacus and psoas muscles share the same tendon and have the same action on the thigh, they are often described as a single muscle ('iliopsoas').
The gluteus minimus muscle is a small muscle originating from the external iliac fossa and inserting on the anterior aspect of the greater trochanter.
If the femur is fixed, the gluteus minimus muscle assists in flexion (bilateral contraction).
The actions of the gluteus minimus muscle reinforce those of the anterior part of the gluteus medius.
In addition to abduction of the thigh, the gluteus minimus muscle assists in flexion and medial rotation.
The gluteus minimus muscle also acts in lateral flexion, or lateral rotation of the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
The innervation of the gluteus minimus muscle is the superior gluteal nerve (L4-S1).
The gluteus medius muscle has a broad origin on the external iliac fossa.
The fibers of the gluteus medius muscle converge and insert on the lateral aspect of the greater trochanter.
When the hip is fixed, the major action of the gluteus medius muscle is abduction of the hip, but it can also assist in flexion (anterior fibers) and extension (posterior fibers).
When the femur is fixed, the gluteus medius muscle is involved in both flexion and extension of the pelvis, depending on whether the anterior or posterior fibers contract (bilateral contraction).
The main action of the gluteus medius muscle is visible when a person stands on one leg: It mostly acts in lateral flexion of the pelvis.
The innervation of the gluteus medius muscle is the superior gluteal nerve (L4-L5).
With unilateral contraction, the gluteus medius muscle acts in lateral flexion of the pelvis, and also stabilizes the pelvis during walking or standing on one foot.
The quadriceps femoris is a massive muscle having four bodies: rectus femoris, vastus lateralis, vastus medialis, and vastus intermedius.
The quadriceps tendon formed by the four quadriceps muscle bodies inserts on and surrounds the patella, then continues as the patellar ligament to insert on the tibial tuberosity.
Vastus intermedius, the deepest of the quadriceps muscle bodies, originates from the upper two-thirds of the anterior femoral shaft.
Vastus intermedius fibers follow the axis of the femur.
Vastus lateralis and medialis arise from either side of the linea aspera on the posterior femoral shaft, then wrap around the sides to meet anteriorly, superficial to vastus intermedius.
Rectus femoris arises from the anterior inferior iliac spine and part of the ilium near the acetabulum, and passes superficial to the three vasti to insert on the common tendon.
Unlike the vasti, rectus femoris crosses both the hip and the knee.
The quadriceps femoris is innervated by the femoral nerve (L2-L4).
Vastus medialis originates on the medial surface of the femur, whereas vastus lateralis originates on the lateral surface of the femur.
The quadriceps femoris extends the knee when its four bodies act together.
The quadriceps femoris is the strongest muscle in the body.
When the knee is flexed, vastus medialis and lateralis can play a small part in rotating the tibia in their respective directions.
When the knee is extended, vastus medialis and lateralis act to stabilize the knee against lateral displacement, complementing the actions of the ligaments.
Rectus femoris acts on both the hip and knee joints.
If the pelvis is fixed, rectus femoris flexes the hip and extends the knee (e.g., in walking).
If the femur is fixed, rectus femoris acts in flexion of the pelvis and extends the knee.
To stretch rectus femoris, the hip must be in extension and the knee in flexion.
When the rectus femoris is shortened, it is often one of the muscles responsible for a flexed hip position, i.e., flexion of the pelvis.
The three vasti can be stretched by full flexion of the knee and hip.
The sartorius is the longest muscle in the body.
The sartorius originates from the ASIS and runs anteromedially down the thigh superficial and medial to the quadriceps.
The sartorius inserts on the superomedial shaft of the tibia.
If the pelvis is fixed, the sartorius flexes, laterally rotates, and abducts the femur, and flexes and medially rotates the tibia.
If the leg is fixed, the sartorius anteverts the pelvis (bilateral contraction) and anteverts, medially rotates, and laterally flexes the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
The quadriceps femoris is innervated by the femoral nerve (L1-L3).
The hamstrings are a group of three posterior muscles working together to flex the knee and extend the thigh.
The hamstrings arise from the ischium, posterior to the hip bone, and insert on the bones of the lower leg.
Two of the hamstring muscles are located medially and insert on the tibia.
The semitendinosus is located posterior to the semimembranosus and inserts via a very long, thin tendon to the superomedial tibial shaft.
The semimembranosus inserts on the posteromedial aspect of the tibial condyle.
The hamstrings are innervated by the common tibial nerve (L5-S2).
The biceps femoris is located laterally and has two heads that merge inferiorly and insert via a common tendon to the head of the fibula.
The tendon of the biceps femoris is bifurcated by the lateral collateral ligament of the knee.
The hamstrings are polyarticular muscles of the hip and knee, i.e., they cross and act on more than one joint.
The primary actions of the hamstrings are extension of the thigh (especially from a flexed position) and flexion of the knee.
The biceps femoris acts in lateral rotation.
The semimembranosus and semitendinosus medially rotate the knee.
If the thigh and leg are fixed, the hamstrings act in extension of the pelvis.
In the flexed knee, the tendons of the hamstrings delimit the popliteal fossa, which is readily visible posteriorly.
Touching the toes causes flexion of the hip and extension of the knees, which stretches the hamstrings.
When the hamstrings are tight, touching the toes is very difficult and may prevent a person from touching the floor when bending down from a standing position.
Tight hamstrings can lead to increased flexion of the lumbar region and indirectly to disc problems at that level.
It is important to be aware of the problem of tight hamstrings and do appropriate warm-up exercises on the floor, especially for beginners.
The hamstrings tend to pull the pelvis into extension and thereby straighten out the lordosis (curvature) of the lumbar spine.
Pectineus runs from the lateral pubis to a line connecting the lesser trochanter to the linea aspera of the femur.
Innervation of the Pectineus is the femoral nerve (L2-L3) and the obturator nerve (L2-L4).
Adductor brevis runs from the medial pubis to the middle part of the linea aspera.
Adductor longus originates even more medially on the pubis.
Adductor longus runs anterior to adductor brevis (almost completely covering it) and inserts lower on the linea aspera.
Innervation of the Adductor brevis and Adductor longus is the obturator nerve (L2-L4).
The adductors are a group of five muscles having their bodies on the medial thigh.
Gracilis is a long, thin, superficial, comparatively weak muscle running from the inferomedial pubis to a spot on the tibial shaft just below the medial condyle.
Gracilis is biarticular, crossing the hip and femur.
Innervation of Gracilis is the obturator nerve (L2-L4).
Adductor magnus, the largest and strongest of the adductor group, is really a compound muscle innervated by two different spinal nerves.
The anterior portion of Adductor magnus originates from the ischiopubic ramus, runs inferomedially, and has a very broad insertion on the linea aspera.
The posterior portion of Adductor magnus originates from the ischial tuberosity, runs straight down, and inserts just above the medial femoral condyle.
Innervation of Adductor magnus is the obturator nerve and the sciatic nerve (L3-L5).
The primary action of the hip adductor group is hip adduction.
To a lesser degree, the hip adductor group can act as hip flexors, lateral rotators, or medial rotators from anatomical position.
If the hip is in a flexed position, the hip adductors act as extensors.
Gracilis, which is polyarticular, can also flex and medially rotate the knee.

If the femur is fixed, the adductors are involved in flexion, medial flexion, lateral rotation, or (in the case of gracilis and the posterior portion of adductor magnus) medial rotation of the pelvis.
These muscles, especially gracilis, are frequently strained or torn during movements involving sudden or extreme abduction of the thigh.
Tensor fasciae latae originates from the anterior iliac crest (near the ASIS) and runs inferiorly and slightly posteriorly.
Tensor fasciae latae inserts on the fascia lata or iliotibial tract, which runs down the lateral thigh and attaches to the superolateral tibia and head of the fibula.
Tensor fasciae latae abducts, flexes, and medially rotates the thigh.
Tensor fasciae latae plays a small part in knee extension or lateral rotation of the flexed knee.
If the thigh and leg are fixed, tensor fasciae latae acts in flexion (bilateral contraction), lateral flexion, or lateral rotation of the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
Gluteus maximus has two layers: a deep layer and a superficial layer.
Gluteus maximus arises on the posterior sacrum and coccyx as well as the posterolateral iliac fossa.
The deep layer of gluteus maximus inserts on the superior linea aspera of the femur, while the superficial layer inserts on the fascia lata.
When the hip bone is fixed, the deep layer of gluteus maximus pulls the femur backward (hip extension), into lateral rotation, and slight adduction.

When the femur is fixed, the deep layer of gluteus maximus acts in extension of the pelvis (bilateral contraction) and in extension, medial rotation, and medial flexion of the pelvis (unilateral contraction).
Gluteus maximus is one of the largest and strongest muscles of the body.
The superficial layer of gluteus maximus (in back) and tensor fasciae latae (in front) insert onto the iliotibial tract from opposite directions.
The superficial layer of gluteus maximus acts on the femur by extending, externally rotating, and abducting it.
The tensor fasciae latae flexes, medially rotates, and abducts the femur.
They assist gluteus medius in maintaining the position of the contralateral pelvis while standing on one foot.
When these two muscles work together, they abduct the hip.
If the femur is fixed, they assist in lateral flexion of the pelvis.
Most muscles acting on the knee also act on the hip and have been described above.
The short head of biceps femoris arises from the femoral shaft.
The short head of biceps femoris merges with the long head of the biceps at the common tendon, which inserts on the head of the fibula.
The short head of biceps femoris flexes and laterally rotates the knee.
The short head of biceps femoris is innervated by the common peroneal nerve (S1-S2).
The popliteus originates from the lateral aspect of the lateral femoral condyle, runs inferomedially, and inserts on a triangular area of the posterosuperomedial tibial shaft.
The popliteus flexes and medially rotates the knee.
The popliteus is innervated by the tibial nerve (L4-S1).
The gastrocnemius are part of the triceps muscles of the calf and are discussed in detail with the muscles of the ankle on page 292.
The gastrocnemius flexes the knee.
Flexion of the hip is produced by the psoas, iliacus, rectus femoris, tensor fasciae latae, gluteus minimus and medius (anterior part), and adductor longus & brevis.
Extension of the hip is produced by the gluteus maximus, biceps femoris (long head), semimembranosus, semitendinosus, and gluteus medius (posterior part).
Abduction of the hip is produced by the gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, tensor fasciae latae, gluteus maximus (superficial part).
Adduction of hip is performed by the adductor magnus, adductor brevis, adductor longus, pectineus, gracilis, psoas, and iliacus.
The biceps femoris (long head) and gluteus maximus (deep part) also adduct the hip, but are not shown in the list.
Medial rotation of the hip is performed by the gluteus medius, gluteus minimus, and tensor fasciae latae.
Lateral rotation of the hip is performed by the gluteus maximus.
The piriformis, obturators, gemelli, quadratus femoris, and biceps femoris (long head) also laterally rotate the hip, but are not shown in the list.
Flexion of the knee is performed by the semitendinosus, semimembranosus, biceps femoris (long head), and popliteus.
The gastrocnemius (medial and lateral) also flexes the knee, but are not shown in the list.
The sartorius and gracilis also flex the knee, but are not shown in the list.
Extension of the knee is performed by the quadriceps femoris, tensor fasciae latae, and gluteus maximus (superficial part).
Medial rotation of the knee is performed by the sartorius, semitendinosus, semimembranosus, and gracilis.
The popliteus also medially rotates the knee, but is not shown in the list.
Lateral rotation of the knee is performed by the tensor fasciae latae, gluteus maximus (superficial part), and biceps femoris (long and short heads).
The foot serves a double function: bearing the weight of the entire body and performing the dynamic movements necessary for walking.
The foot contains 26 bones, 31 joints, and 20 intrinsic muscles.
Malformation of the foot is common due to mechanical stresses from excess weight and poorly-fitted shoes.
The ankle joint combines the malleability of the foot with the strength of the leg bones.
The muscles which act on the ankle joint also act on the foot.
The medial arch and proximal phalanges do not normally come in contact with the ground.
The heel, lateral arch, distal metatarsal region, and distal phalanges do contact the ground.
A skeletal top view of the foot shows three regions (from front to back): the forefoot, the hindfoot, and the midfoot.
The forefoot consists of five slender bones arranged next to each other and flaring outward like 'spokes' (numbered I-V from medial to lateral).
Each spoke consists of a metatarsal which is extended by a phalange (toe).
The hindfoot or posterior tarsus consists of two sizeable bones sitting on top of each other: the talus and the calcaneus.
The midfoot or anterior tarsus consists of five small bones: the navicular, the cuboid, and three cuneiforms.
The area between the front and back of the foot is a junction zone where a lot of torsion occurs.
The bony structure of the foot looks fork-shaped, with a 'lateral foot' following the calcaneus and lengthening into two lateral spokes (IV and V).
The 'lateral foot' is involved in receiving the body's weight.
The 'medial foot' follows the talus and lengthens into three medial spokes (I-III).
The 'medial foot' is involved in propulsion.
Dorsiflexion, also called flexion, is a decrease in the angle between the superior (dorsal) surface of the foot and the anterior leg.
The range of motion (ROM) for dorsiflexion is greater when the knee is flexed and less when the knee is extended.
There is greater tension in the gastrocnemius when the knee is extended.
Plantar flexion, also called extension, is an increase in the angle between the dorsal surface of the foot and the anterior leg.
Dorsiflexion and plantar flexion occur mainly at the ankle joint.
A combination of adduction and plantar flexion, in which the sole is directed toward the median plane, is called supination.
A combination of abduction and dorsiflexion, in which the sole of the foot is directed away from the median plane, is called pronation.
In abduction and adduction, the distal end of the foot moves away from and toward the median plane, respectively.
These movements can be amplified by or confused with medial and lateral rotation of the hip when the knee is extended or rotation of the flexed knee.
You will notice movement of the tibial tuberosity in these situations.
Adduction, supination, and plantar flexion combine to produce inversion.
Abduction, pronation, and dorsiflexion combine to produce eversion.
These combined movements of the foot are due to the bony surfaces and orientation of the axes of movement.
They are executed simultaneously while walking.
Two long bones form the skeleton of the leg: the tibia and fibula.
The fibula, on the lateral side of the leg, is a thin bone, triangular in cross section.
The fibula is twisted on itself and its edges are therefore not really straight.
This gives the bone a certain suppleness, i.e., its curvature can be slightly modified.
The fibula consists of three main parts: the head, shaft, and lateral malleolus.
The tibia, on the medial side, is also triangular in cross section, with three surfaces and three edges.
Both the distal and proximal ends of the tibia are large.
The proximal end of the tibia is part of the knee joint.
The anterior edge of the tibia bifurcates superiorly and inferiorly.
The distal end of the tibia is massive in size and ends in the medial malleolus, which has anterior and posterior edges, and a peak.

The tibia and fibula contact each other at two points: proximally, they form a synovial joint consisting of an oval surface on the head of the fibula and a corresponding surface posterior to the lateral part of the tibial plateau.
The joint is held together in a capsule, reinforced by anterior and posterior ligaments.
Along the length of their shafts, the tibia and fibula are connected by the interosseous membrane, which runs from the medial surface of the fibula to the lateral edge of the tibia.
Distally, it is a syndesmosis (fibrous joint) in which the two bones are connected by fibrous tissue, but no cartilage.
The bones are held together by anterior and posterior ligaments.
When the ankle moves, the two bones are simultaneously stable and mobile.
Distally, they 'clasp' the talus (the uppermost tarsal bone).
The ankle joint resembles a pincer or crescent wrench gripping a section of a hemisphere when viewed from the front.
The lateral and medial malleoli, and distal tibia fit against the three facets of the talar body.
Seen from the side, the cartilaginous superior and inferior articulating surfaces of the ankle joint appear concave and convex, respectively.
The two malleoli provide a snug fit around the talus.
The lateral malleolus extends farther down and is more obliquely oriented than the medial malleolus.
In cross section, there is a slight ridge on the articulating surface of the distal tibia and a corresponding groove on the talus.
The superior talus is wider anteriorly than posteriorly.
Because of the tight fit of the bones, the ankle is a pure hinge joint capable only of dorsiflexion and plantar flexion.
The axis of movement of the ankle passes through the two malleoli.
Of all the joints in the foot, the ankle allows the greatest range of motion (ROM).
In dorsiflexion, the anterior (wider) part of the superior talus moves into the 'pincer,' and the joint is more stable.
In plantar flexion, the posterior (narrower) part of the talus is in the 'pincer,' and the joint is therefore less stable.
The ankle joint is held together by a capsule which attaches to the proximal joint surfaces of the tibia, fibula, and talus.
The capsule is slack anteriorly and posteriorly, allowing for plantar flexion and dorsiflexion of the foot.
The ankle joint is primarily reinforced by lateral ligaments, which are arranged roughly symmetrically.
Three ligamentous fasciae on each side arise from a malleolus and run obliquely down the two bones of the hindfoot.
Laterally, the anterior and posterior talofibular ligaments end on the talus and tie it directly to the bones of the leg, while the calcaneofibular ligament runs down to the lateral calcaneus.
Medially, the medial collateral ligament (or deltoid ligament) consists of three fasciae in two layers.
The superficial layer of the medial collateral ligament has fibers that insert on the navicular bone, calcaneonavicular ligament, and sustentaculum tali.
The deep layer of the medial collateral ligament has anterior fibers that insert on the talus and posterior fibers that insert on the anteromedial side of the talus.
Depending on the position of the ankle, the ligaments become more or less taut.
In dorsiflexion, the posterior ligaments are taut, and the anterior ones are slack.
The reverse is true in plantar flexion.
Since the ankle is least stable in plantar flexion, a 'sprained ankle' occurs most commonly in this position.
The anterior talofibular ligament is the one most often injured.
The stability of the ankle is also assisted by muscular action, which helps adjust the 'pincer' action, making it more or less tight, during active movement of the ankle.
The talus and calcaneus are the most posterior and massive of the tarsal bones.
The calcaneus articulates with the heel region.
The talus articulates with the ankle.
The talus and calcaneus are like two rectangular boxes set at an angle on top of each other.
The talus points medially and the calcaneus points laterally.
The talus and calcaneus each have six surfaces: superior, inferior, medial, lateral, anterior, and posterior.
The talus articulates with the tibia and fibula above, the calcaneus below, and the navicular in front.
No muscle inserts on the talus.
The talus is moved indirectly via the structures surrounding it.
The calcaneus articulates with the talus above and the cuboid in front.
The calcaneus has a prominent projection called the sustentaculum tali which helps support the talus.
The calcaneus has a groove for passage of various tendons, blood vessels, and nerves.
The calcaneus has a large insertion area for the Achilles tendon.
The posterior surface of the talus constitutes the back of its trochlea.
Inferiorly, the talus has two lateral tubercles separated by a groove where the flexor hallucis longus tendon passes.
The sinus tarsi is a groove between the talus and calcaneus bones.
The sinus tarsi contains the interosseous talocalcaneal ligament and blood vessels.
The talus sits slightly obliquely on the calcaneus because the long axes of the two bones are oriented medially and laterally, respectively.
Anteriorly, the convex surface of the talus fits against the concave surface of the calcaneus.
Posteriorly, the concave surface of the talus fits against the convex surface of the calcaneus.
The subtalar joint sits below the ankle at a vertical angle.
The subtalar joint has greater range of motion (ROM) than the ankle, but its ROM is still limited.
In the frontal plane (back view, with support), the calcaneus tilts sideways below the talus.
In the sagittal plane (without support), the calcaneus moves front to back.
In the transverse plane (view from above), the calcaneus moves while turning under the talus.
The axis of Henke runs obliquely from back to front, bottom to top, and lateral to medial.
The axis of Henke enters the posterolateral tuberosity of the calcaneus, runs anterosuperomedially, and exits through the medial neck of the talus.
Inversion occurs around the axis of Henke and is a combination of adduction, supination, and plantar flexion.
Eversion is the opposite movement of inversion and is a combination of abduction, pronation, and dorsiflexion.
The long axes of the two articulating surfaces and the sinus tarsi are directed anterolaterally.
The surfaces are held together by two capsules: posteriorly, a capsule which attaches to the circumference of the surfaces and anteriorly, a capsule shared with the transverse tarsal joint.
Because the surfaces (on the talus) and the capsules are continuous, the movements of the anterior subtalar and the transverse tarsal joints are inseparable.
The interosseous talocalcaneal ligament forms a double row of ligaments passing along the tarsal tunnel.
There is also an anterior and a posterior ligament.
Between the talus and calcaneus, and the metatarsals, are five small bones collectively called the anterior tarsals.
The anterior tarsals correspond to the portion of the external foot called the "instep."
Navicular articulates with talus.
Medially, the navicular has an externally palpable tubercle for insertion of tibialis posterior.
Cuboid does not actually bear much resemblance to a cube.
Cuboid has facets for articulation with calcaneus (proximally), lateral cuneiform and navicular (medially), and metatarsals IV and V (distally).
A proximal process of the cuboid fits under calcaneus and helps maintain the lateral arch of the foot.
A lateral notch of the cuboid continues as a groove on the inferior surface and accommodates the tendon of peroneus longus.
The cuboid is concave on the proximal end and convex on the distal end, where there are three facets for articulation with the cuneiforms.
The anterior tarsals, and their many gliding joints, allow a reasonable degree of flexibility, though less than that of the corresponding wrist bones.
The cuneiforms are three small wedge-shaped bones with the sharp edges directed inferiorly.
The cuneiforms articulate proximally with navicular and distally with metatarsals I—III.
Together with cuboid and the metatarsals, they constitute the transverse arch of the foot.
The transverse tarsal joint (Chopart's joint) is located between the posterior and medial foot.
The transverse tarsal joint comprises the articulation of the calcaneus and cuboid bones laterally, and the articulation of the talus and navicular bones medially.
The lateral part of the transverse tarsal joint is lower and involves an S-shaped surface of calcaneus (concave medially, convex laterally) fitting against a corresponding surface of cuboid.
The medial part of the transverse tarsal joint is higher and involves a convex surface of the talar head fitting against a concave surface of navicular.
Seen from above, the transverse tarsal joint is curved rather than straight.
The basic movements of the midtarsal joint are inversion and eversion.
The dominant movement in the midtarsal joint is abduction-adduction.
The transverse tarsal joint is reinforced superiorly by the talonavicular and calcaneocuboidal ligaments.
A medial capsule unites with the capsule of the anterior subtalar joint.
A lateral capsule unites the calcaneus with the cuboid bone.
The bifurcate ligament runs from the calcaneus and spreads out vertically on the navicular and horizontally on the cuboid.
The surfaces of the navicular and cuboid bones are roughly perpendicular at their junction.
The transverse tarsal joint is reinforced inferiorly by three ligaments.
The two layers of the plantar calcaneocuboid (short plantar) ligament run from the calcaneus to the proximal cuboid and to the base of the metatarsals.
The long plantar ligament runs from the calcaneus to a ridge on the cuboid, passes over a groove, and continues on to attach to the bases of metatarsals II-V.
The long plantar ligament is quite strong and helps support the arches of the foot.
The plantar calcaneonavicular ligament runs from the sustentaculum tali to the navicular and helps support the talar head.
The metatarsals and phalanges are five parallel 'spokes' made of small bones that flare out anteriorly.
Each spoke consists of a metatarsal and phalanges, which form the skeleton of the toe.
Each metatarsal consists of a proximal base, a body, and a distal head.
The base of the metatarsal is roughly quadrangular with facets for articulation with the tarsals and adjacent metatarsals.
The body of the metatarsal is triangular in cross section.
The head of the metatarsal is convexly rounded with a cartilaginous surface for articulation with the proximal phalanx and a tiny tubercle on each side.
The proximal phalanx of each toe has a concavely rounded base for articulation with the metatarsal and a pulley-shaped head.
The base of the middle phalanx is concave but with a median crest to match the shape of the head of the proximal phalanx.
The head of the distal phalanx is flared to support the toenail superiorly and has an inferior tubercle to support the fleshy part of the toe.
The tarsometatarsal joints are formed between the tarsal and metatarsal bones.
The articular line joins the distal cuneiform and cuboid bones with the proximal bases of the metatarsal bones.
The junction of the tarsometatarsal joints is irregular, not straight.
The articular surfaces of the tarsometatarsal joints allow for small gliding movements between the bones.
The tarsometatarsal joints have some mobility, but it is quite limited.
Plantar flexion and dorsiflexion are the possible movements at the tarsometatarsal joints.
The range of motion at the tarsometatarsal joints is in the increasing order II, III, I, IV, V.
The second metatarsal has very little mobility and represents the axis of movement for pronation and supination of the foot.
The tarsometatarsal joints are supported by capsules, each of which is connected to neighboring capsules.
The capsules of the tarsometatarsal joints are reinforced by many small ligaments, dorsal and plantar, which serve to connect the bones to one another.
At each metatarsophalangeal joint, the head of the metatarsal articulates with the base of the proximal phalanx.
The articular surfaces at each metatarsophalangeal joint have the shape of a small condyle, which allows movement in all three planes.
Dorsiflexion has greater range of motion than plantar flexion.
The cartilage of the metatarsal is more developed on the dorsal side.
The muscles associated with dorsiflexion are stronger.
The proximal interphalangeal joint is a hinge that allows plantar flexion but not dorsiflexion.
Strong dorsiflexion is needed for going uphill or up the stairs.
The distal interphalangeal joint is a hinge that allows both plantar and dorsiflexion.
The first spoke (metatarsal I) includes the big toe and has two phalanges instead of three.
The metatarsal and phalanges in the first spoke are larger than in toes II-V.
The big toe plays an important role in walking or running, especially in the digitigrade phase.
The disproportionate size of metatarsal I can lead to instability or medial pain when on tiptoe or during prolonged walks.
Two small sesamoid bones are located in the plantar cartilage on the head of metatarsal I and act as shock absorbers during weightbearing.
Ligaments of the metatarsophalangeal and interphalangeal joints have the same general plan.
The joints are held by a capsule, which is attached to the neighboring structures and reinforced by ligaments.
The extensor digitorum brevis is the only dorsal intrinsic muscle of the foot.
The extensor digitorum brevis arises from the anterosuperolateral calcaneus and divides into four bodies.
The medial tendon of the extensor digitorum brevis inserts on the proximal phalanx I, while the other three merge laterally with the tendons of the extensor digitorum longus, inserting on toes II-IV.
The actions of the extensor digitorum brevis are dorsiflexion of toes I-IV, especially at the level of the proximal phalanx; it reinforces the action of the extensor digitorum longus.
The extensor digitorum brevis is innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (S1-S2).
Intrinsic muscles of the foot can be subdivided into three groups: middle, medial, and lateral.
The interossei form the fourth (deepest) layer of the middle group and occupy the spaces between the metatarsals, where they originate.
There are four dorsal interossei (arising closer to the dorsal surface of the foot) and three plantar interossei (closer to the plantar surface).
The interossei insert at the base of the proximal phalanges (plantar side) and at the extensor digitorum longus tendon (dorsal side).
The principal action of the interossei is plantar flexion of proximal phalanges, which is important in the propulsion phase of walking.
The interossei are innervated by the lateral plantar nerve (S1-S2).
By contracting on one side or the other, the interossei help spread the toes apart or pull them back together.
The origins of the interossei help prevent the metatarsals from spreading apart and maintain the transverse arch of the foot.
The lumbricals are four small muscles (in the second layer) running from the flexor digitorum longus tendons to the dorsal parts of the extensor digitorum longus tendons.
The lumbricals mostly 'fine tune' the actions of other toe muscles.
The lumbricals are innervated by the medial and lateral plantar nerves (L5-S2).

The quadratus plantae (also called flexor digitorum accessorius) arises from the body of the calcaneus and inserts on the posterolateral border of the flexor digitorum longus tendon near its division into four parts.
The quadratus plantae belongs to the second layer.
The actions of the quadratus plantae are redirecting the pull of the flexor digitorum longus tendons to be more in line with the axes of the toes.
The quadratus plantae is innervated by the lateral plantar nerve (S1-S2).
The flexor digitorum brevis is part of the first (most superficial) layer.
The flexor digitorum brevis arises from the posteroinferior tuberosity of the calcaneus, splits into four parts, and inserts laterally on middle phalanges II-V.
Each tendon of the flexor digitorum brevis is 'perforated' to allow passage of the flexor digitorum longus tendons to the distal phalanges.

The action of the flexor digitorum brevis is plantar flexion of middle and proximal phalanges of toes II-V; it is often responsible for the condition called 'clawfoot,' particularly when the action of the interossei is weak.
The flexor digitorum brevis is innervated by the medial plantar nerve (L5-S1).
There are three muscles which insert on the proximal phalanx of the big toe and, in passing, on the sesamoid bone.
The Flexor hallucis brevis (third layer) originates from the cuboid and the two lateral cuneiforms and inserts via two tendons on either side of proximal phalanx I.
The action of the Flexor hallucis brevis is plantar flexion of the proximal phalanx of the big toe.
The Flexor hallucis brevis is innervated by the medial plantar nerve (L5-S1).
The Adductor hallucis has two layers: the oblique adductor arises on the cuboid, and the transverse adductor on the metatarsophalangeal joints III-V.
Both layers of the Adductor hallucis merge in a tendon, which inserts on the lateral base of proximal phalanx I.

The Adductor hallucis is the muscle primarily responsible for 'hallux valgus', a malformation in which metatarsal I is permanently adducted and proximal phalanx I abducted, such that the big toe overlaps the second toe.
The action of the Adductor hallucis is adduction of the big toe.
The Adductor hallucis is innervated by the medial plantar nerve (S1-S2).
The Abductor hallucis is the most superficial of the three muscles.
The Abductor hallucis originates at the medial tuberosity on the medial surface of the calcaneus and inserts on the medial base of the proximal phalanx I.
The Abductor hallucis actively supports the medial arch and assists in keeping the big toe properly aligned by opposing 'hallux valgus'.
The actions of the Abductor hallucis are abduction of the big toe and assistance in plantar flexion of the metatarsophalangeal joint.
The Abductor hallucis is innervated by the lateral plantar nerve (L5-S1).
When we speak of adduction or abduction of toes, the reference is the axis of the 2ND toe, not the median plane of the body.
There are three small muscles on the lateral side of the foot.
The Flexor digiti minimi brevis (third layer) arises from the base of metatarsal V and inserts on the base of proximal phalanx V.
The Abductor digiti minimi (first layer) originates from the posteroinferior calcaneus and inserts laterally on the base of proximal phalanx V.
The action of the Abductor digiti minimi is abduction and plantar flexion of the little toe; it also supports the lateral arch.
The Abductor digiti minimi is innervated by the lateral plantar nerve (S1-S2).
The Opponens digiti minimi originates from the cuboid and inserts on the lateral side of metatarsal V.
The actions of the Opponens digiti minimi are orienting metatarsal V toward the other metatarsals and resisting the spreading of the anterior portion of the foot.
The Opponens digiti minimi is innervated by the lateral plantar nerve (S1-S2).
On the anterior surface of the leg are three long muscles whose tendons run anterior to the ankle, where they are held in place by the extensor retinaculum.

The Tibialis anterior originates from the lateral condyle and superolateral shaft of tibia, passes under the extensor retinaculum, and inserts on the medial cuneiform (inferomedial surface) and base of metatarsal I.
The Tibialis anterior is the strongest dorsiflexor.
The actions of the Tibialis anterior are dorsiflexion of the foot and also supination (lifts the medial edge of the foot by pulling on anterior tarsals).
The Tibialis anterior is innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (L4-S1).
The Extensor hallucis longus arises from the central medial fibula and interosseous membrane, passes under the extensor retinaculum, and inserts dorsally on distal phalanx I.
The actions of the Extensor hallucis longus are dorsiflexion of the big toe and foot and also supination (lifts the medial edge of the foot).
The Extensor hallucis longus is innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (L4-S1).
Extensor digitorum longus originates from the lateral tibial condyle, most of the anterior fibular shaft, and interosseous membrane.
The tendon of extensor digitorum longus passes under the extensor retinaculum, splits into four parts, and inserts on toes II-V.
Each of the four tendons of extensor digitorum longus further splits into two slips attaching to the sides of the middle phalanx, and a central slip attaching to the base of the distal phalanx.
The action of extensor digitorum longus is dorsiflexion of toes II-V, foot, and ankle; it mainly acts on the proximal phalanx and is one of the muscles responsible for the clawing action of the toes.
Extensor digitorum longus is innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (L4-S1).
Short muscles of the foot that insert on the tendon of extensor digitorum longus complement its action: extensor digitorum brevis (p. 281) and interossei (p. 282).
Peroneus tertius is an insignificant muscle, absent in some individuals.
Peroneus tertius arises from the anteroinferior fibula and inserts on metatarsal V.
The actions of peroneus tertius are dorsiflexion and eversion of the foot.
Peroneus tertius is innervated by the deep peroneal nerve (L5-S1).
There are two muscles on the lateral side of the leg that attach to the fibula: the peroneus muscles.
Peroneus longus arises from the head and superolateral shaft of the fibula.

The tendon of peroneus longus follows a complicated path behind the lateral malleolus, under the peroneal retinaculum, inferior to the peroneal tubercle of calcaneus, along the groove of the plantar (inferior) cuboid, and finally inserts inferiorly on the base of the medial cuneiform and base of metatarsal I.
Peroneus brevis arises from the inferolateral fibular shaft, where it is covered by peroneus longus.
The tendon of peroneus brevis passes behind the lateral malleolus, under the peroneal retinaculum, superior to the peroneal tubercle, and inserts on the lateral tubercle of metatarsal V.
The actions of peroneus longus are pronation (lifts lateral edge of foot and lowers medial edge); plantar flexes ankle.
Peroneus longus is innervated by the superficial peroneal nerve (L5-S1).
The actions of peroneus brevis are pronation (lifts lateral edge of foot); assists in plantar flexion and abduction of foot.
Peroneus brevis is innervated by the superficial peroneal nerve (L5-S1).
The tendons of tibialis posterior and peroneus longus, coming from opposite sides, form a sling under the middle part of the foot which is crucial in supporting the arches.
Peroneus longus and brevis both strengthen and support the lateral arch, stabilizing the ankle and preventing loss of balance laterally, especially when standing on one foot or on tiptoe.
The posterior group of leg muscles is the most important muscle group.
The deeper layer of the posterior group of leg muscles consists of three muscles located on the posterior surfaces of the tibia and fibula.

The Flexor digitorum longus originates from the posteromedial tibial shaft, runs posterior to the medial malleolus and sustentaculum tali, along the plantar surface of the foot, and inserts on distal phalanges II-V.
Tibialis posterior is the deepest calf muscle.
Tibialis posterior arises from the posterosuperior tibial and fibular shafts and interosseous membrane, and passes posterior to the medial malleolus and anterior to sustentaculum tali.
The primary insertion of tibialis posterior is on a prominent medial tubercle of navicular, but it also inserts on the cuboid, lateral cuneiform, and metatarsals II-IV.
Actions of tibialis posterior: plantar flexion of toes II-V and ankle, inversion of foot, support of arches.
Tibialis posterior is the most powerful flexor of toes II-V.
Tibialis posterior also assists in plantar flexion, supination, and adduction of the foot, the latter counteracted by quadratus plantae.
Innervation of tibialis posterior: tibial nerve (S1-S3).
The role of tibialis posterior, in conjunction with peroneus longus, in forming a 'sling' for the middle foot, was noted on the preceding page.
Innervation of peroneus longus: tibial nerve (L4-L5).

Flexor hallucis longus arises from the posteroinferior fibula and interosseous membrane, runs posterior to the medial malleolus, along a groove on the posterior talus, behind sustentaculum tali, along the medial plantar surface of the foot, and inserts on distal phalanx I.
Flexor hallucis longus is important in the propulsion phase of walking.
Actions of flexor hallucis longus: plantar flexion of big toe and ankle, inversion, support of medial arch; assists in plantar flexion and adduction of foot.
Flexor hallucis longus has a very important role in stabilizing the foot when standing on tiptoe, since the pushing action of the big toe offsets the anterior loss of balance.
Flexor hallucis longus also helps stabilize the ankle.
Innervation of flexor hallucis longus: tibial nerve (S1-S3).
The superficial group of posterior calf muscles is known collectively as the triceps surae.
Triceps surae is the strongest muscle group of the leg.
Triceps surae consists of three muscular heads, all of which terminate on the Achilles tendon, which then attaches to the posterior surface of the calcaneus.
Soleus is the deepest head of triceps surae.
Soleus arises from the posterosuperior tibia and fibula.
Soleus crosses two joints: the ankle and the subtalar joint.
Innervation of soleus: common tibial nerve (L5/S2).
Actions of triceps surae: Together, the three muscles pull the calcaneus into plantar flexion under the talus, with a tendency to inversion.
Soleus is covered by the two superficial heads of gastrocnemius, which originate at the distal posterior femur from a tendon attaching to the back of each condyle.
Gastrocnemius shapes the outline of the posterior calf.
Gastrocnemius crosses three articulations: the ankle, subtalar joint, and knee.
Innervation of gastrocnemius: common tibial nerve (S1-S2).
Gastrocnemius indirectly pulls the talus into plantar flexion. This second action is actually more important than the first because it gives the joint more mobility.
The gastrocnemius muscle crosses the knee.
The position of the knee affects the gastrocnemius' efficiency as a plantar flexor of the ankle.
Plantar flexion of the ankle by the triceps surae is necessary but not sufficient for standing on tiptoe.
Dorsiflexion at the ankle stretches the soleus muscle.
When the knee is very flexed, the gastrocnemius is slack and inefficient as an ankle flexor.
When the knee is extended or slightly flexed, the gastrocnemius is more taut and efficient as an ankle flexor.
To stretch the gastrocnemius, knee extension is required.
When the knee is flexed and the leg and foot bear the body's weight, the gastrocnemius and hamstrings combine their forces to become knee extensors.
When the foot is not bearing the body's weight, the gastrocnemius and hamstrings act as knee flexors.
The tibialis anterior, extensor hallucis longus, and extensor digitorum longus tendons, passing anterior to the bimalleolar axis, perform dorsiflexion.
The peroneus tertius tendon also performs dorsiflexion.
The peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, triceps surae, flexor hallucis longus, and tibialis posterior tendons, passing posterior to the bimalleolar axis, perform plantar flexion.

The extensor hallucis longus, tibialis anterior, tibialis posterior, flexor digitorum longus, and flexor hallucis longus tendons, passing medial to the longitudinal axis of the foot, perform inversion/adduction.
The triceps surae also contributes to inversion.
The peroneus longus and brevis, peroneus tertius, and extensor digitorum longus (lateral part) tendons, passing lateral to the longitudinal axis of the foot, perform eversion/abduction.
Plantar flexion is dominant over dorsiflexion, and inversion/adduction is dominant over eversion/abduction.
The talus fits more snugly into the 'pincer' formed by the distal tibia and fibula during dorsiflexion than plantar flexion.
The peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, flexor hallucis longus, and tibialis posterior muscles pull down the fibula to compensate for the loss of stability during plantar flexion.
The fit of the 'pincer' on the plantar-flexed talus is improved when the lateral malleolus is lowered.
The flexor hallucis longus and tibialis posterior muscles tighten the two sides of the 'pincer'.
When the fibula is lowered, the distal tibiofibular ligaments come under tension, tightening the 'pincer'.
These effects on the 'pincer' by muscles and ligaments stabilize the ankle during plantar flexion.
The foot has three arches.
The arches of the foot rest on three points of support.
The arches of the foot constitute a flexible slat that serves as a shock absorber and adapts the foot's shape to the ground.
When standing, the weight of the body is distributed among three points.
The posteroinferior tuberosity of the calcaneus receives most of the weight.
The head of metatarsal I is the secondary weight-bearing point.
The head of metatarsal V supports the least weight.

The medial arch is formed primarily by five bones (calcaneus, talus, navicular, medial cuneiform, metatarsal I), four ligaments (talocalcaneal, calcaneonavicular, and small ligaments joining the cuneiform to navicular and metatarsal), and four muscles (abductor hallucis, tibialis posterior, peroneus longus, flexor hallucis longus).

Flexor hallucis longus has three functions with respect to the medial arch: 1. stretches the arch like the string of a bow 2. supports the calcaneus by passing under the sustentaculum tali 3. supports the talus by passing along its posterior groove.

In architecture, a supporting structure in the shape of a triangle (a truss) has the following stress distribution: the weight on the top part causes compression stress (on the top) and stretching stress (on the bottom). Due to the relative elasticity of the lower part, a very heavy weight can be supported.
The lateral arch is not as high as the medial one.

The lateral arch is formed primarily by three bones (calcaneus, cuboid, metatarsal V), three ligaments (short plantar [calcaneocuboid] ligament, long plantar ligament, plantar aponeurosis), and two muscles (peroneus brevis, peroneus longus).
Peroneus longus has two functions here: supports the calcaneus by passing under the peroneal tubercle supports the cuboid.
The transverse arch is most visible around the middle of the metatarsals.
The transverse arch is higher on the medial (navicular) side than the lateral (cuboid) side.
The transverse arch's muscular support comes primarily from adductor hallucis (transverse head), peroneus longus, tibialis posterior, and the interossei.
The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings.
Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats.
A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
